# Polaris Documentation

> User and developer documentation for Polaris Finance — the self-scaling stablecoin operating system. Mint pAssets backed entirely by onchain collateral and yield. No T-bills. No CEXs. No compromises.

## Introduction

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/

# Welcome to Polaris

Polaris is the **pETH-powered yield layer for all of DeFi**, a self-scaling stablecoin operating system. It produces yield-bearing **pETH** and uses it as the base collateral for minting synthetic assets (**pAssets**) through onchain debt positions.

The infrastructure is trustless and immutable, and every pAsset is backed entirely by onchain collateral and yield with no T-bill or CEX dependency. The result is a competitive, risk-adjusted yield source for both ETH and stablecoins. And through **Flows**, third-party protocols can build on top of Polaris assets and share in protocol yield.

This documentation explains how the system works, how to use it, and the trade-offs it makes.

## Launch timeline

<style>{`
  /* ===== Launch timeline · "The Voyage Chart" =======================
     The launch milestones plotted as a constellation, the sailed route
     tracing toward Polaris (Testnet 2, the brightest star, "you are
     here"). Rendered straight on the page, no panel, so it never stacks
     a second dark block above the system diagram. Colors adapt to the
     light cream page and the dark navy page. */

  .pl-voyage { margin: 1.1rem 0 2.75rem; }
  .pl-chart {
    position: relative; width: 100%;
    height: 168px; min-width: 700px; max-width: 860px;
  }

  /* The route, drawn in SVG so it can trace itself on entry. Warm gold +
     navy on the cream page; starlight + blue on navy. */
  .pl-route { position: absolute; inset: 0; width: 100%; height: 100%; overflow: visible; }
  .pl-route path { fill: none; stroke-linecap: round; stroke-linejoin: round; }
  .pl-route-glow {
    stroke: #b1934f; stroke-width: 6; opacity: 0.1; filter: blur(3px);
    stroke-dasharray: 100; stroke-dashoffset: 0;
  }
  :is(.dark) .pl-route-glow { stroke: #d8c9a4; opacity: 0.16; }
  .pl-route-line {
    stroke: url(#pl-grad-l); stroke-width: 2.4;
    stroke-dasharray: 100; stroke-dashoffset: 0;
  }
  :is(.dark) .pl-route-line { stroke: url(#pl-grad-d); }
  .pl-route-ahead { stroke: rgba(60,80,110,0.55); stroke-width: 1.7; stroke-dasharray: 1.5 7; }
  :is(.dark) .pl-route-ahead { stroke: rgba(123,165,201,0.5); }

  /* Nodes share an x-position; star + label hang off a single rail line.
     Evenly spaced for a deliberate rhythm; every star sits on the rail. */
  .pl-node { position: absolute; top: 0; bottom: 0; width: 0; }
  .pl-n1 { left: 10%; } .pl-n2 { left: 30%; } .pl-n3 { left: 50%; }
  .pl-n4 { left: 70%; } .pl-n5 { left: 90%; }

  }

  /* Polaris, the brightest star, "you are here". */
  .pl-polaris { display: block; width: 40px; height: 40px; }
  .pl-polaris span { position: absolute; left: 50%; top: 50%; }

    width: 56px; height: 56px; transform: translate(-50%,-50%); border-radius: 50%;
    background: radial-gradient(circle,
      rgba(63,127,168,0.24) 0%, rgba(199,154,78,0.13) 48%, rgba(199,154,78,0) 72%);
    animation: pl-twinkle 4s ease-in-out infinite;
  }
  :is(.dark) .pl-halo {
    width: 64px; height: 64px;
    background: radial-gradient(circle,
      rgba(232,220,196,0.42) 0%, rgba(123,165,201,0.24) 42%, rgba(123,165,201,0) 72%);

    background: linear-gradient(90deg, transparent, rgba(199,154,78,0.8) 48%, transparent); }

    background: linear-gradient(0deg, transparent, rgba(199,154,78,0.8) 50%, transparent); }

    animation: pl-badge-pulse 2.8s ease-in-out infinite;
  }
  :is(.dark) .pl-badge {
    color: #abccea; background: rgba(123,165,201,0.18); border-color: rgba(123,165,201,0.5);
  }

  /* ---- Signature entrance: the route draws itself toward Polaris,
       stars igniting as it reaches them. Plays once. ---- */
  .pl-route-line, .pl-route-glow { animation: pl-draw 1.5s cubic-bezier(0.22,0.61,0.18,1) 0.15s both; }
  .pl-route-ahead { animation: pl-fade 0.8s ease 1.65s both; }
  .pl-spark, .pl-polaris-star, .pl-polaris-core, .pl-distant span {
    animation: pl-ignite 0.7s cubic-bezier(0.2,0.7,0.2,1) both;
  }
  .pl-s1 .pl-spark { animation-delay: 0.35s; }
  .pl-s2 .pl-spark { animation-delay: 0.7s; }
  .pl-s3 .pl-spark { animation-delay: 1.05s; }
  .pl-polaris-star, .pl-polaris-core { animation-delay: 1.4s; }
  .pl-distant span { animation-delay: 1.8s; }
  .pl-flare-h, .pl-flare-v { animation: pl-flare 0.9s ease-out 1.45s both; }
  .pl-halo { animation: pl-halo-in 0.9s ease-out 1.4s both, pl-twinkle 4s ease-in-out 2.3s infinite; }
  .pl-label .pl-name, .pl-label .pl-date { animation: pl-rise 0.6s ease-out both; }
  .pl-n1 .pl-name, .pl-n1 .pl-date { animation-delay: 0.55s; }
  .pl-n2 .pl-name, .pl-n2 .pl-date { animation-delay: 0.9s; }
  .pl-n3 .pl-name, .pl-n3 .pl-date { animation-delay: 1.2s; }
  .pl-n4 .pl-name, .pl-n4 .pl-date { animation-delay: 1.55s; }
  .pl-n5 .pl-name, .pl-n5 .pl-date { animation-delay: 1.85s; }

  @keyframes pl-draw { from { stroke-dashoffset: 100; } to { stroke-dashoffset: 0; } }
  @keyframes pl-fade { from { opacity: 0; } to { opacity: 1; } }
  @keyframes pl-ignite {
    0%   { opacity: 0; transform: translate(-50%,-50%) scale(0.2) rotate(-25deg); }
    100% { opacity: 1; transform: translate(-50%,-50%) scale(1) rotate(0); }
  }
  @keyframes pl-flare {
    0%   { opacity: 0; transform: translate(-50%,-50%) scale(0.3); }
    60%  { opacity: 1; }
    100% { opacity: 1; transform: translate(-50%,-50%) scale(1); }
  }
  @keyframes pl-halo-in {
    from { opacity: 0; transform: translate(-50%,-50%) scale(0.4); }
    to   { opacity: 1; transform: translate(-50%,-50%) scale(1); }
  }
  @keyframes pl-twinkle {
    0%,100% { opacity: 0.6; transform: translate(-50%,-50%) scale(1); }
    50%     { opacity: 1;   transform: translate(-50%,-50%) scale(1.12); }
  }
  @keyframes pl-rise { from { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(7px); } to { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(0); } }
  @keyframes pl-badge-pulse {
    0%,100% { box-shadow: 0 0 0 0 rgba(123,165,201,0); }
    50%     { box-shadow: 0 0 0 3px rgba(123,165,201,0.12); }
  }

  /* Below the desktop breakpoint the horizontal route can't fit its labels,
     so the chart becomes a vertical meridian: every milestone, including the
     "you are here" Polaris star, stays visible with no horizontal scroll. */
  @media (max-width: 1023px) {
    .pl-voyage { overflow-x: visible; }
    .pl-chart {
      position: relative; min-width: 0; width: 100%; max-width: 420px;
      height: auto; display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 0;
    }
    .pl-route { display: none; }
    .pl-chart::before {
      content: ''; position: absolute; left: 13px; top: 24px; bottom: 24px;
      width: 2px; border-radius: 2px;
      background: linear-gradient(180deg, #2c4a6e 0%, #b1934f 55%, #3f7fa8 100%);
    }
    :is(.dark) .pl-chart::before {
      background: linear-gradient(180deg, #214a72 0%, #d8c9a4 55%, #7ba5c9 100%);
    }
    .pl-node {
      position: relative; left: auto; top: auto; bottom: auto;
      width: 100%; min-height: 46px; display: flex; align-items: center;
    }
    .pl-n4 { min-height: 64px; }
    .pl-star { left: 14px; top: 50%; }
    .pl-label {
      position: relative; left: 40px; transform: none;
      width: auto; height: auto; align-items: flex-start; text-align: left;
    }
    .pl-above, .pl-below { top: auto; bottom: auto; height: auto; }
  }

  @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) {
    .pl-route-line, .pl-route-glow, .pl-route-ahead,
    .pl-spark, .pl-polaris-star, .pl-polaris-core, .pl-distant span,
    .pl-flare-h, .pl-flare-v, .pl-halo, .pl-badge,
    .pl-label .pl-name, .pl-label .pl-date { animation: none; }
  }
`}</style>

<div style={{maxWidth: '860px', margin: '2rem 0'}}>
  ![Polaris system overview: ETH swaps into pETH on the bonding curve, pETH collateralizes CDPs that mint pUSD, and burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor and releases ETH](/polaris-system-v2.svg)
</div>

## The three engines

Polaris is made of three products that each stand on their own, but whose real power is the synergy between them. The bonding curve makes pETH work. The CDP makes pAssets work. The conversion mechanism and POLAR make them work together:

| Engine | What it is | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| **1.&nbsp;pETH** | ETH deposited into a two-way **bonding curve**, with a floor price that only rises | A capital-efficient, yield-bearing collateral with guaranteed liquidity |
| **2.&nbsp;pAssets** | Synthetic pegged (USD, Gold, etc.) assets minted against collateral, via a Collateralized Debt Position (CDP) | Counterparty-free stables that scale with onchain activity |
| **3.&nbsp;POLAR** | The stewardship token, minted only by burning pETH in a **conversion auction** | Aligns long-term incentives and steers the protocol within hardcoded bounds |

## Timed Explainers

<style>{`
  .pl-depth-grid {
    display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr);
    gap: 0.75rem; max-width: 880px; margin: 0.75rem 0 1.9rem;
  }
  @media (max-width: 700px) {
    .pl-depth-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; }
  }

  .pl-depth-card {
    display: flex; flex-direction: column;
    text-decoration: none !important;
    border: 1px solid rgba(44,74,110,0.16); border-radius: 10px;
    padding: 1.05rem 1.15rem 0.95rem;
    background: rgba(44,74,110,0.018);
    transition: border-color 0.18s ease, background 0.18s ease,
                box-shadow 0.18s ease,
                transform 0.18s cubic-bezier(0.2,0.7,0.2,1);
  }
  :is(.dark) .pl-depth-card {
    border-color: rgba(212,200,174,0.13);
    background: rgba(212,200,174,0.012);
  }
  /* The deepest path carries a quiet primary emphasis */
  .pl-depth-deep { border-color: rgba(63,127,168,0.32); }
  :is(.dark) .pl-depth-deep { border-color: rgba(123,165,201,0.3); }

  .pl-depth-card:hover {
    border-color: rgba(63,127,168,0.5);
    background: rgba(63,127,168,0.05);
    transform: translateY(-3px);
    box-shadow: 0 12px 28px -18px rgba(44,74,110,0.45);
  }
  :is(.dark) .pl-depth-card:hover {
    border-color: rgba(123,165,201,0.46);
    background: rgba(123,165,201,0.06);
    box-shadow: 0 14px 32px -18px rgba(0,0,0,0.72);
  }

    display: flex; align-items: flex-start; justify-content: space-between;
    gap: 0.75rem; margin-bottom: 0.85rem;

    font-size: 0.58rem; font-weight: 600; letter-spacing: 0.14em;
    text-transform: uppercase; color: #46647f; padding-bottom: 2px;
  }
  :is(.dark) .pl-depth-unit { color: #7d96ab; }

  /* Depth meter: a mini-meridian with a star at how far in you go */
  .pl-depth-meter { position: relative; width: 58px; height: 12px; margin-top: 5px; flex-shrink: 0; }
  .pl-depth-track {
    position: absolute; top: 50%; left: 0; right: 0; height: 2px;
    transform: translateY(-50%); border-radius: 2px; background: rgba(44,74,110,0.16);
  }
  :is(.dark) .pl-depth-track { background: rgba(212,200,174,0.14); }
  .pl-depth-fill {
    position: absolute; top: 50%; left: 0; height: 2px;
    transform: translateY(-50%); border-radius: 2px;
    background: linear-gradient(90deg, #b1934f, #3f7fa8);
  }
  :is(.dark) .pl-depth-fill { background: linear-gradient(90deg, #d8c9a4, #7ba5c9); }
  .pl-depth-mark {
    position: absolute; top: 50%; width: 9px; height: 9px;
    transform: translate(-50%, -50%); background: #3f7fa8;
    clip-path: polygon(50% 0%, 60% 40%, 100% 50%, 60% 60%, 50% 100%, 40% 60%, 0% 50%, 40% 40%);
  }
  :is(.dark) .pl-depth-mark { background: #d8c9a4; }
  .pl-depth-f1 { width: 34%; } .pl-depth-m1 { left: 34%; }
  .pl-depth-f2 { width: 67%; } .pl-depth-m2 { left: 67%; }
  .pl-depth-f3 { width: 100%; } .pl-depth-m3 { left: 100%; }
  .pl-depth-card:hover .pl-depth-mark { animation: pl-depth-twinkle 1.7s ease-in-out infinite; }

  .pl-depth-title {
    font-size: 0.9rem; font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: 0.005em;
    color: #1a2e42; margin-bottom: 0.32rem;
  }
  :is(.dark) .pl-depth-title { color: #ece3cf; }
  .pl-depth-desc {
    font-size: 0.76rem; line-height: 1.5; color: #46647f; margin-bottom: 0.95rem;
  }
  :is(.dark) .pl-depth-desc { color: #8ea2b4; }

  .pl-depth-foot {
    display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: flex-end;
    margin-top: auto; padding-top: 0.72rem;
    border-top: 1px solid rgba(44,74,110,0.1);
  }
  :is(.dark) .pl-depth-foot { border-top-color: rgba(212,200,174,0.1); }
  .pl-depth-go {
    display: inline-flex; align-items: center; gap: 4px;
    font-size: 0.72rem; font-weight: 600; color: #2c5f8a;
    opacity: 0.9; transition: opacity 0.18s ease;
  }
  :is(.dark) .pl-depth-go { color: #9cc2e0; }
  .pl-depth-arrow { transition: transform 0.18s cubic-bezier(0.2,0.7,0.2,1); }
  .pl-depth-card:hover .pl-depth-go { opacity: 1; }
  .pl-depth-card:hover .pl-depth-arrow { transform: translateX(3px); }

  @keyframes pl-depth-twinkle {
    0%, 100% { transform: translate(-50%, -50%) scale(1);    opacity: 0.85; }
    50%      { transform: translate(-50%, -50%) scale(1.28); opacity: 1; }
  }
  @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) {
    .pl-depth-card:hover .pl-depth-mark { animation: none; }
  }
`}</style>

<div className="pl-depth-grid">
  <a href="/explainers/2-min" className="pl-depth-card">
    <div className="pl-depth-head">
      <span className="pl-depth-time">
        <span className="pl-depth-num">2</span>
        <span className="pl-depth-unit">min</span>
      </span>
      <span className="pl-depth-meter">
        <span className="pl-depth-track" />
        <span className="pl-depth-fill pl-depth-f1" />
        <span className="pl-depth-mark pl-depth-m1" />
      </span>
    </div>
    <span className="pl-depth-title">Overview</span>
    <span className="pl-depth-desc">What Polaris is, in one sitting.</span>
    <span className="pl-depth-foot">
      <span className="pl-depth-go">Read <span className="pl-depth-arrow">→</span></span>
    </span>
  </a>

  <a href="/explainers/5-min" className="pl-depth-card">
    <div className="pl-depth-head">
      <span className="pl-depth-time">
        <span className="pl-depth-num">5</span>
        <span className="pl-depth-unit">min</span>
      </span>

        <span className="pl-depth-track" />

    <span className="pl-depth-desc">Three engines and the flywheel.</span>

      <span className="pl-depth-go">Read <span className="pl-depth-arrow">→</span></span>

## A note on this documentation

Polaris is a developing protocol still in testnet. This documentation reflects as accurately as possible the state of the current testnet (v2).

---

## Changelog

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/changelog

# Changelog

This is a changelog for **this documentation**, not for the Polaris protocol. It records what changed in these pages — structure, content, and accessibility — so returning readers can see what is new. It does not invent protocol release history; deployed addresses, audits, and launch parameters remain *forthcoming* until launch.

Entries follow [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com), use ISO dates (YYYY-MM-DD), and are listed newest first.

## [Unreleased]

Nothing yet.

## [2026-06-08]

A documentation best-practices pass that upgrades the existing pages without changing the protocol model.

### Added

- Point-of-decision risk notes on the action pages (open a trove, redemptions, Stability Pool deposits, conversions), each linking to the full Risk Disclosure.
- A POLAR tokenomics page covering distribution, allocations, and vesting.
- The Polaris system overview diagram on Core Concepts and the lineage diagram on Why Polaris, each with descriptive alt text, a caption, and a numbered text description so the meaning survives without the image.
- Developer **Gotchas** and **Failure modes** sections, plus a worked integration code example, in the developer pages.
- An `llms.txt` machine-readable export so integrators and agents can consume the docs programmatically.
- This documentation changelog.

### Changed

- Enriched the six role paths from thin link lists into role-specific reading sequences, each with a "Who this is for" line, prerequisites, and a one-line note on why each step matters.
- Added audience signposting so developers and users are routed to the right starting point.
- Accessibility pass across the affected pages, including image alternatives and text descriptions for every embedded diagram.
- Trimmed over-long meta descriptions to keep search snippets from truncating.

---

## Core Concepts

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/core-concepts

# Core Concepts

This page is the map. Each term is defined in one or two sentences and links to its full treatment. If you read nothing else, read this.

## The assets

- **pAsset**: a synthetic asset minted by Polaris (pUSD, pETH, pGOLD, pCHF, …). Each is overcollateralized and counterparty-free. See [The pAsset Catalog](/minting/passet-catalog).
- **pUSD**: the flagship pAsset, pegged to the US dollar, backed by pETH collateral. See [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets).
- **pETH**: ETH deposited into a two-way [bonding curve](/peth/bonding-curve). It is the *only* collateral Polaris accepts, has a floor price that only rises, and is yield-bearing.
- **POLAR**: the protocol's stewardship token. New POLAR can be minted *only* by burning pETH in a [conversion auction](/polar/conversion-auctions). **vePOLAR** is POLAR locked for stewardship rights.

## Minting & positions

- **CDP** (Collateralized Debt Position): the mechanism behind minting. You post collateral and mint the pAsset directly from the protocol, not borrowing another user's funds. See [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets).
- **Trove**: your individual borrowing position in a Polaris CDP. Each trove is isolated, with its own collateral, debt, and collateralization ratio. See [Managing Your Trove](/minting/managing-your-trove).
- **StablecoinOS**: Polaris is a *factory* of CDP instances, each issuing a different pAsset but all sharing the same pETH collateral and bonding curve.

## Keeping the peg

- **Redemption**: exchanging a pAsset for its underlying collateral at face value (minus a fee) when the pAsset trades *below* peg. Restores the floor. See [Redemptions](/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions).
- **Minting (mechanism)**: the inverse of redemption. Arbitrageurs add pETH to troves to mint pAssets when the pAsset trades *above* peg. Restores the ceiling.
- **Autonomous interest rates**: redemption and minting volume are used as onchain proxy signals for the peg, letting Polaris set rates without an external price oracle.

## Risk states

- **Liquidation**: if a trove falls below its minimum collateralization ratio, it is liquidated, with the [Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool) absorbing the debt. See [Liquidations](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations).
- **Recovery Mode**: a protocol-wide protective state triggered when the [Total Collateralization Ratio (TCR)](/resources/glossary) drops below a threshold. See [Recovery Mode](/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode).
- **Floor price / floor ratio**: the mathematically enforced minimum price of pETH (in ETH), and that floor as a percentage of market price (your maximum drawdown). See [The Floor Price](/peth/floor-price).

## Yield & coordination

- **Stability Pool**: a reserve of pAssets that processes liquidations and is the primary venue for earning yield. See [The Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool).
- **Conversion auction**: the mechanism that mints POLAR by burning pETH; the most protocol-synergetic action possible. See [Conversion Auctions](/polar/conversion-auctions).
- **Fee Router**: receives pETH from conversions and swap fees, then distributes yield to troves and vePOLAR lockers. See [The Fee Router](/stewardship/fee-router).
- **Stewardship**: vePOLAR holders steer *quantitative parameters within hardcoded bounds*. They do not govern the immutable core. See [Stewardship, not Governance](/stewardship/stewardship).

## The shape of the system

A single primitive, the bonding curve, produces pETH. pETH collateralizes every pAsset. Activity on any pAsset generates fees; fees burn pETH and raise its floor; a rising floor strengthens every position. POLAR captures the long-term value of that flywheel and steers it.

![Polaris system overview: ETH swaps into pETH on the bonding curve, pETH collateralizes CDPs that mint pUSD, and burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor and releases ETH](/polaris-system-v2.svg)

*The Polaris system at a glance, end to end.*

The diagram traces four steps and an outcome:

1. **Swap.** ETH enters the **bonding curve** and is issued as **pETH**; the curve captures volatility.
2. **Collateral.** pETH is deposited as the sole collateral for **CDPs**.
3. **Borrow.** A CDP mints **pUSD** (or another pAsset) against that pETH, for scalable onchain yield.
4. **Stewardship.** Burning pETH in a conversion auction mints **POLAR**, which raises the pETH floor price and releases ETH.

> Adoption ⇒ activity ⇒ velocity ⇒ fees ⇒ yield ⇒ more demand.

Ready to use it? Start with [Getting Started](/getting-started), or jump to [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets).

---

## Getting Started

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/getting-started

# Getting Started

This page is the shortest path from "I have ETH" to "I am using Polaris." Each step links to its full treatment.

## Choose your path

> Building on Polaris? Jump to [Architecture](/developers/architecture) and the [Develop](/paths/develop) path. Just using Polaris? Stay here and pick a journey below.

If you already know what you are here to do, start with the matching journey:

- **Borrow pAssets** — get pETH, open a trove, mint a pAsset, and manage liquidation risk: [Borrow pAssets](/paths/borrow-passets).
- **Earn yield** — deposit pAssets into a Stability Pool or understand Fee Router yield: [Earn Yield](/paths/earn-yield).
- **Hold or use pETH** — understand the bonding curve, floor price, and external collateral use: [Hold or Use pETH](/paths/hold-use-peth).
- **Convert and lock POLAR** — burn pETH to mint POLAR, lock as vePOLAR, and participate in bounded stewardship: [Convert and Lock POLAR](/paths/convert-lock-polar).
- **Develop** — plan an integration, indexer, vault, analytics surface, or risk tool: [Develop](/paths/develop).
- **Safety and verification** — confirm official app, contracts, audits, and launch status before interacting: [Safety and Verification](/paths/safety-verification).

## What you need

- **An Ethereum wallet** — any standard EOA or smart-account wallet.
- **ETH on the supported network** — the exact network is *forthcoming* and will be confirmed at launch. ETH is the only asset you need to begin; Polaris converts it to [pETH](/peth/bonding-curve) for you.

That is the whole list. No KYC, no whitelist, no offchain account.

## The basic flow

In one line: **deposit ETH ⇒ receive pETH ⇒ open a trove and mint a pAsset ⇒ optionally deposit to the Stability Pool to earn yield.** Step by step:

1. **Deposit ETH.** Connect your wallet and deposit ETH into the protocol.
2. **Receive pETH from the [bonding curve](/peth/bonding-curve).** Your ETH is swapped for pETH at the curve's current price. pETH is an ETH proxy with a floor price that only rises, and it is the only collateral Polaris accepts.
3. **Open a [trove](/minting/open-a-trove) and mint a pAsset.** Post your pETH as collateral, choose which pAsset to mint (pUSD, pGOLD, …) and how much. The pAsset lands in your wallet; the debt stays in your trove. See [the catalog](/minting/passet-catalog) for what you can mint.
4. **Optionally, earn yield.** Deposit your pAsset into the [Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool) to earn a share of borrower interest plus discounted pETH from liquidations. This step is optional — you can hold, LP, or use your pAsset anywhere in DeFi instead.

## Where the app lives

> **Note:** The Polaris app URL is *forthcoming* and will be published here at launch. Until then, treat any "Polaris" frontend you find as unverified.

## Launch verification

Before you connect a wallet or sign a transaction, verify:

- The app URL is linked from this documentation or another official Polaris channel.
- The supported network matches the network listed here at launch.
- Contract addresses match [Contracts and Addresses](/resources/contracts).
- Verified source, ABIs, and audit status match [Contract Reference](/developers/contract-reference) and [Audits and Security](/resources/audits-security).
- The product page you are following has been updated from conceptual flow to launch parameters.

## Where to go next

- New to the model? Read [Core Concepts](/core-concepts) for the one-page map, or [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets) for how the CDP works.
- Ready to mint? Go straight to [Open a Trove](/minting/open-a-trove).
- Want to understand the collateral? Start with [The Bonding Curve](/peth/bonding-curve) and [The Floor Price](/peth/floor-price).
- Here for the yield? See [The Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool).

---

## Why Polaris

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/why-polaris

# Why Polaris

## The trilemma

Stablecoins are said to be **scalable, yield-bearing, or trustless — pick two**. Market structure has mostly reinforced that framing: the largest stablecoins rely on issuers, custodians, offchain assets, or other counterparties, while the cleaner trustless designs have remained smaller.

That trade-off is real, but it is not a law of nature. It exists because, for years, high offchain interest rates made it *easy* to back a stablecoin with T-bills and CEX yield and bring that yield onchain. Easy, but not trustless — and not the innovative path.

## The degradation pattern

The deeper problem is **structural drift toward centralization**. Stablecoins tend to start relatively clean and get dirtier in how they source yield as they grow, because centralized backing is the path of least resistance for scaling.

| Stablecoin | Where it falls short |
|---|---|
| **USDC** | Centralized issuer with freeze/blacklist powers; holders do not receive the issuer's reserve yield directly; stress events have shown that banking dependencies matter. |
| **USDS / DAI (Sky)** | Began with cleaner crypto-collateral roots, then added centralized stablecoin and real-world-asset exposure as it scaled. Governance-set rates remain. |
| **USDe** | Uses market-neutral and custody/counterparty-dependent infrastructure; scaling can require more counterparties, venues, and operational trust assumptions. |
| **LUSD / BOLD** | Genuinely trustless and counterparty-free — but limited yield sources and capital inefficiency keep them in a trustless-focused niche. Great to hold, less attractive to mint. |

The lesson: a stablecoin built for trustlessness must be *architected* so the degradation cannot happen, not merely intend to stay clean.

## The counterparty-free lineage

Polaris descends from the line of immutable, counterparty-free CDP stablecoins, and is built to fix the limitation each prior generation hit.

![Polaris lineage: SAI (2017) ⇒ LUSD (2021) ⇒ BOLD / Liquity V2 (2024) ⇒ pUSD (2026)](/infographics/polaris-lineage.svg)

*The counterparty-free CDP lineage, 2017 to present.*

In text, the lineage reads:

1. **2017 — SAI:** the first CDP stablecoin, single-collateral. It later upgraded to DAI and then drifted toward centralized backing.
2. **2021 — LUSD:** immutable and decentralized, but capital-inefficient, and it spent roughly 20% of its life more than 1% above peg.
3. **2024 — BOLD (Liquity V2):** a refined CDP mechanism from the original Liquity developers — still trustless, still yield-constrained.
4. **2026 — pUSD:** the next chapter. A bonding-curve collateral design that solves the overpeg problem and unlocks onchain-scaling yield without adding a counterparty.

## How Polaris resolves it

Polaris is built so the only counterparty you trust is **immutable code and Ethereum**:

- **Onchain-only yield.** Every yield source — borrower interest, bonding curve swap fees, conversion gains, PolarEX swap fees — is internal to the protocol and grows with activity. Nothing is rented from a CEX or a T-bill desk.
- **Immutable core.** The bonding curve, CDP logic, liquidations, redemptions, and the Stability Pool are fixed in code. No admin key can add a PSM or a freeze function under pressure.
- **Counterparty-free by construction.** pUSD is designed in the same counterparty-free lineage as LUSD and BOLD — but adds yield that *scales* with DeFi volume rather than capping growth at what a counterparty can deliver.

> Having a counterparty means capping your growth to whatever yield that counterparty can deliver.

Polaris turns onchain trading volume itself into a stablecoin yield source through its bonding curve, and extends the model to *any* asset through the [StablecoinOS](/core-concepts). DeFi has known uncensorable-but-small and censorable-but-scalable stablecoins. Polaris is built to be the uncensorable, scalable one.

---

<BlogPostCard
  url="https://polarisfinance.io/blog/why-polaris"
  title="Why Polaris?"
  description="Analyzing where USDC, USDS, USDe, and LUSD fall short — and how pUSD delivers scalable, yield-bearing stability without counterparty risk."
  readingTime={6}
  image="https://polarisfinance.io/polaris-og.png"
/>

---

## Borrow pAssets

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/paths/borrow-passets

# Borrow pAssets

**Who this is for:** anyone who wants to mint pUSD, pGOLD, or another pAsset against pETH collateral and actively manage that position.

**Prerequisites:** an Ethereum wallet, ETH on the supported network, and a working grasp of overcollateralization and liquidation. If those terms are new, read [Core Concepts](/core-concepts) first.

> **Launch status:** The public app URL, supported network, deployed addresses, final collateral thresholds, and exact UI labels are not published yet. Do not connect to a frontend or contract that claims to be Polaris unless it is linked from these docs or another official Polaris channel at launch.

## Reading sequence

Read these in order — each step builds the context the next one assumes.

1. [Getting Started](/getting-started) — confirms your wallet, network, and launch status so you do not interact with an unverified deployment.
2. [The Bonding Curve](/peth/bonding-curve) — pETH is the *only* collateral Polaris accepts; you need to hold it before you can borrow.
3. [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets) — explains the CDP model and the peg mechanics that determine your interest rate.
4. [Open a Trove](/minting/open-a-trove) — the conceptual flow for posting collateral and minting your pAsset.
5. [Managing Your Trove](/minting/managing-your-trove) — how collateralization, debt, and interest move over time, and how to stay clear of liquidation.
6. [Liquidations](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations) and [Recovery Mode](/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode) — the failure states that decide how conservatively to size your position.

## What to verify at launch

- The official app URL.
- The supported network.
- The pETH, pAsset, trove manager, and Stability Pool addresses.
- Final minimum collateralization, liquidation, redemption, and fee parameters for the pAsset you mint.
- Audit reports and verified contract source.

## Next

- Want yield on the pAsset after minting? Continue to [Earn Yield](/paths/earn-yield).
- Want to understand the collateral first? Continue to [Hold or Use pETH](/paths/hold-use-peth).
- Want the safety checklist? Continue to [Safety and Verification](/paths/safety-verification).

---

## Convert and Lock POLAR

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/paths/convert-lock-polar

# Convert and Lock POLAR

**Who this is for:** long-term participants who want to acquire POLAR through conversion, lock it as vePOLAR, and steer the protocol within its hardcoded bounds.

**Prerequisites:** pETH to burn in a conversion auction, and acceptance that conversion is a one-way action — you cannot un-burn pETH. Read [The Floor Price](/peth/floor-price) first if you want to understand what your burn contributes.

> **Launch status:** The conversion UI, POLAR and vePOLAR addresses, lock durations, voting process, and final parameter bounds are pending audited deployment. Do not use an unofficial conversion or locking interface.

## Reading sequence

Read these in order — understand the token and the irreversible burn before you commit pETH.

1. [The POLAR Token](/polar/polar-token) — POLAR's role and why it can be minted *only* by burning pETH.
2. [Conversion Auctions](/polar/conversion-auctions) — the mechanism and its risks; read this before burning any pETH to mint POLAR.
3. [Participate in a Conversion](/polar/participate-in-conversion) — the conceptual user flow for a conversion.
4. [vePOLAR](/stewardship/vepolar) — locking durations, the rights locking grants, and its limitations.
5. [Stewardship, not Governance](/stewardship/stewardship) — the hardcoded bounds your vote operates within; read before voting on any proposal.

## What vePOLAR can and cannot do

vePOLAR can steward bounded parameters, vote on permitted onboarding decisions, and receive a stewarded share of protocol yield through the Fee Router. It cannot upgrade contracts, change the bonding curve, change liquidation or redemption logic, create emissions gauges, or mint POLAR except through the pETH conversion mechanism.

## What to verify at launch

- The official POLAR, conversion auction, and vePOLAR addresses.
- Verified source and ABI for each contract.
- Final lock duration choices and vote-power decay rules.
- Final proposal, voting, quorum, timelock, and execution rules.
- The hardcoded parameter bounds for any proposal you vote on.

---

## Develop

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/paths/develop

# Develop

**Who this is for:** engineers building an app, indexer, risk system, vault, liquidation monitor, analytics surface, or external market around Polaris.

**Prerequisites:** familiarity with onchain reads/writes (viem, ethers, or equivalent) and the Polaris product model. If the concepts are new, read [Core Concepts](/core-concepts) before the architecture pages.

> **Launch status:** Polaris is in pre-deployment documentation mode. Contract addresses, ABIs, verified source, canonical events, SDKs, subgraphs, and API endpoints are not published yet. Build against the documented model, but do not hardcode pre-launch addresses, method names, or event signatures.

## Reading sequence

Read these in order — model the system before you bind to any artifact.

1. [Architecture](/developers/architecture) — the component map and integration assumptions you design against.
2. [Contract Reference](/developers/contract-reference) — the planned address, ABI, and event publication format, plus the gotchas and failure modes integrators hit.
3. [Integration Guide](/developers/integration-guide) — read paths, data indexing, and the worked integration example.
4. [Contracts and Addresses](/resources/contracts) — the canonical registry to verify before pointing any production system at a deployment.
5. [Audits and Security](/resources/audits-security) — the security model and audit status; read before treating the deployment as production-grade.

## Build now, bind later

Until deployment artifacts are published, the safest useful work is interface planning:

- Model the product concepts: pETH, pAssets, troves, Stability Pools, Fee Router, POLAR, and vePOLAR.
- Design data schemas around stable conceptual entities, not unpublished function names.
- Build verification gates so your app refuses unverified networks or addresses.
- Keep chain-specific config external and replaceable.
- Track the official docs for the deployment manifest, ABIs, and event catalog.

## Next

- Need component boundaries? Start with [Architecture](/developers/architecture).
- Need artifact expectations? Go to [Contract Reference](/developers/contract-reference).
- Need indexing and safety gates? Go to [Integration Guide](/developers/integration-guide).

---

## Earn Yield

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/paths/earn-yield

# Earn Yield

**Who this is for:** holders who want protocol-native yield from pAssets, pETH, or locked POLAR — without token emissions, bribes, or offchain backing.

**Prerequisites:** a pAsset to deposit (so most readers come here after [Borrow pAssets](/paths/borrow-passets)) or pETH, plus an understanding that Stability Pool yield carries liquidation-conversion exposure to your principal.

> **Launch status:** Yield flows are documented conceptually before launch. Exact splits, buffering parameters, app flows, and contract addresses are pending audited deployment.

## Reading sequence

Read these in order — start with where the yield originates before committing capital.

1. [Where the Yield Comes From](/yield/yield-sources) — the full source map, so you can judge whether each yield stream is sustainable rather than subsidized.
2. [The Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool) — the primary venue for pAsset yield, and how liquidations can convert your deposit into discounted pETH.
3. [Deposit to the Stability Pool](/yield/deposit-to-stability-pool) — the user flow you will follow once the app is public.
4. [The Fee Router](/stewardship/fee-router) — how pETH from fees and conversions is distributed to troves and vePOLAR lockers, which determines your non-pool yield.
5. [Flows](/stewardship/flows) — only if you are an integrator or operator seeking protocol revenue sharing; safe to skip as a plain depositor.

## What yield is not

Polaris yield is not a token-emission program, gauge market, bribe market, T-bill wrapper, or CEX lending strategy. It comes from protocol activity: borrower interest, liquidation gains, bonding-curve fees, conversion gains, and other onchain fees described in the yield docs.

## What to verify at launch

- The official app URL and supported network.
- The Stability Pool address for the pAsset you deposit.
- The final interest split and liquidation parameters for that pAsset.
- The Fee Router address and verified source.
- Whether any wrapper vault you use is official, audited, and listed by the project that operates it.

## Next

- Borrow first? Go to [Borrow pAssets](/paths/borrow-passets).
- Lock POLAR for stewarded yield? Go to [Convert and Lock POLAR](/paths/convert-lock-polar).
- Verify contracts before depositing? Go to [Safety and Verification](/paths/safety-verification).

---

## Hold or Use pETH

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/paths/hold-use-peth

# Hold or Use pETH

**Who this is for:** anyone who wants to hold pETH for its rising floor, use it as collateral, or understand the asset before borrowing against it.

**Prerequisites:** ETH on the supported network and a wallet. No trove or pAsset is required to hold pETH; borrowing is optional and comes later.

> **Launch status:** The pETH contract address, bonding-curve deployment, supported network, external integrations, and final curve parameters are pending publication at launch. Do not trust unofficial addresses.

## Reading sequence

Read these in order to understand what pETH is and its downside bound before relying on it.

1. [The Bonding Curve](/peth/bonding-curve) — how ETH deposits become pETH, and where your exit liquidity comes from.
2. [The Floor Price](/peth/floor-price) — the mathematically enforced downside bound in ETH terms; this is your worst case.
3. [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets) — only if you may use pETH inside a trove; it explains what changes when pETH becomes collateral.
4. [Where the Yield Comes From](/yield/yield-sources) — how bonding-curve and conversion activity raises the floor and benefits pETH holders.
5. [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure) — read before using pETH as collateral anywhere, inside or outside Polaris.

## pETH roles

| Role | What to read |
|---|---|
| Hold pETH directly | [Bonding Curve](/peth/bonding-curve), [Floor Price](/peth/floor-price) |
| Borrow against pETH | [Borrow pAssets](/paths/borrow-passets) |
| Integrate pETH externally | [Develop](/paths/develop), [Architecture](/developers/architecture) |
| Verify the asset | [Safety and Verification](/paths/safety-verification) |

## What to verify at launch

- The official pETH contract address and verified source.
- The bonding-curve address and supported network.
- Final curve, fee, and floor-ratio parameters once published.
- The exit path from pETH back to ETH.
- Any external venue's risk controls before using pETH outside Polaris.

---

## Safety and Verification

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/paths/safety-verification

# Safety and Verification

**Who this is for:** every Polaris user and integrator; run this path before connecting a wallet, sending ETH, minting a pAsset, depositing into a pool, locking POLAR, or integrating contracts.

**Prerequisites:** none. This is the path to complete *first*, before any other journey, because pre-launch is exactly when impersonation and phishing are most likely.

> **Launch status:** Official app URLs, deployed addresses, ABIs, verified source, final parameters, and audit reports are pending publication. Until they are published, treat every claimed frontend, token, contract, airdrop, or support message as unverified.

## Verification checklist

Work top to bottom; each row names a check and where it will be confirmed once Polaris launches.

| Check | Where it will be confirmed |
|---|---|
| Official app URL | [Getting Started](/getting-started) |
| Deployed addresses | [Contracts and Addresses](/resources/contracts) |
| Verified source and ABIs | [Contract Reference](/developers/contract-reference) and block explorer links |
| Audit reports | [Audits and Security](/resources/audits-security) |
| Final user parameters | The relevant product page and contract reference |
| Stewardship bounds | [Stewardship, not Governance](/stewardship/stewardship) |

## Things Polaris will not ask for

- A seed phrase or private key.
- KYC for protocol use.
- A direct transfer to a support wallet.
- Interaction with an unpublished address.
- Trust in a frontend that is not linked from official Polaris channels.

## Risk docs

Read [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure) for smart-contract, collateral, liquidation, peg, Stability Pool, pETH, stewardship, and phishing risks. Read [Audits and Security](/resources/audits-security) for the security model and audit status.

## Developer verification

For apps and indexers, add explicit allowlists for network IDs and contract addresses once published. Your integration should fail closed if the deployment manifest, ABI, or verified source does not match the official docs.

---

## Managing Your Trove

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/minting/managing-your-trove

# Managing Your Trove

A trove is a living position. After you [open it](/minting/open-a-trove), you can adjust it freely: add collateral, mint more, repay debt, or close it out. Everything traces back to one number — your **Individual Collateralization Ratio**.

## Individual Collateralization Ratio (ICR)

Your **Individual Collateralization Ratio (ICR)** is the value of your trove's collateral divided by the recorded value of its debt:

> ICR = collateral value ÷ debt face value

Collateral value is calculated through the protocol's collateral pricing path. Debt value is the debt you owe in that pAsset's reference unit, including accrued interest; it is not the temporary market price at which you might buy the pAsset on a DEX. A trove holding pETH worth $15,000 against 10,000 pUSD of debt has an ICR of 150%. Higher is safer. Two thresholds matter:

- Below the **minimum ICR**, the trove can be [liquidated](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations).
- [Redemptions](/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions) draw collateral from every open trove collectively, pro-rata to its recorded collateral and regardless of ICR — a higher ICR protects against liquidation, not redemption.

Your ICR moves on its own as the price of ETH moves — you do not have to touch the trove for it to drift. Managing the trove is mostly about keeping the ICR where you want it.

## Before you adjust a trove

> **Before you act:**
> Check your current ICR, the projected ICR after the transaction, Recovery Mode status, accrued interest, your redemption exposure, and the gas cost of buying or repaying pAssets. If you need to acquire pAsset or pETH first, account for DEX slippage, bonding-curve slippage, MEV, and transaction ordering.

> **Warning:**
> Minting more or withdrawing collateral lowers your ICR. Drop below the minimum and your trove is [liquidated](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations) automatically, and you lose collateral to cover the debt with no margin call. [Redemptions](/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions) can also reach your position while it is still solvent — there is no minimum ICR to cross — shrinking your collateral and pETH exposure proportionally without your consent. See the [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure).

## Parameter status

| Parameter | Status | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum ICR | Pending final per-pAsset values | Defines the liquidation line in Normal Mode |
| Recovery Mode threshold | Pending final value | When system backing is thin, actions that reduce collateral or add debt face a higher minimum ratio; liquidation eligibility itself stays at the normal minimum |
| Borrow-rate formula and bounds | Pending final audited values | Determines how quickly debt grows |
| Minimum debt / dust rules | Pending final values | Can affect partial repayments, partial redemptions, and closing |

## Add collateral

Deposit more pETH into the trove to **raise your ICR** without changing your debt. This is the simplest way to step back from a liquidation or redemption threshold during a drawdown. (Need pETH? Mint it on the [bonding curve](/peth/bonding-curve).)

## Mint more / repay debt

- **Mint more.** If your ICR has room, mint additional pAsset against the same collateral. This *lowers* your ICR, so leave a buffer.
- **Repay debt.** Burn pAsset back into the trove to reduce your debt and *raise* your ICR. Repaying is the other lever for recovering a thinning position, and the only way to eventually withdraw collateral.

## Monitor interest accrual

Borrowers pay an [autonomous, market-aware interest rate](/minting/minting-passets) that accrues continuously against your trove. Interest is added to your debt over time, which means your ICR drifts *down* even if ETH holds flat and you do nothing. Check your accrued debt periodically and repay or top up so accrual does not quietly walk you toward a threshold.

Note that collateral pETH also *earns* yield while it sits in the trove, partly offsetting the interest cost; see [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets) for how the effective rate is composed.

### Worked example: interest accrual

Suppose your trove owes 10,000 pUSD and the app currently quotes an 8% annualized borrow rate. As a simple estimate, before rate changes and compounding:

| Period | Interest estimate | New debt |
|---|---:|---:|
| 30 days | 10,000 × 8% × 30 / 365 = 65.75 pUSD | 10,065.75 pUSD |
| 90 days | 10,000 × 8% × 90 / 365 = 197.26 pUSD | 10,197.26 pUSD |

If your collateral value stays flat at $15,000, the 30-day estimate moves your ICR from 150% to about 149%. The contract may accrue continuously and the rate may change, so use the app's recorded debt for exact decisions.

## Close the trove

Closing is the reverse of opening (**repay debt ⇒ withdraw collateral**):

1. **Repay the full debt.** Burn enough pAsset to clear the recorded debt, including accrued interest. You must hold (or re-acquire) the pAsset to do this.
2. **Withdraw collateral.** With the debt at zero, withdraw your pETH. The trove is closed.

If you do not hold enough of the pAsset to repay (for example, you sold it), you will need to buy it back on the market first.

## How ICR maps to your exposure

| ICR | Liquidation exposure | Redemption exposure |
|---|---|---|
| Comfortably high | Minimal — survives large ETH drops | Low impact — redemptions reduce every trove pro-rata to its collateral, and a large buffer keeps your ICR healthy afterward |
| Moderate | Survives normal volatility | Moderate — a system-wide redemption shrinks your collateral proportionally and can erode your buffer |
| Near the minimum | High — a sharp ETH move liquidates you | A system-wide redemption reduces your collateral pro-rata and, on thin margin, can leave you closer to the liquidation line |

The practical takeaway: a buffer is not wasted capital, it is the price of staying in control of your position. Keep enough margin that ordinary volatility and quiet interest accrual cannot force an outcome you did not choose.

---

## Minting pAssets

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/minting/minting-passets

# Minting pAssets

## The CDP model

Polaris issues pAssets through a **Collateralized Debt Position (CDP)**. From the outside it looks like borrowing against collateral, but under the hood it is different from a money market like Aave.

- A **money market** matches lenders and borrowers through a shared pool. You borrow funds another user supplied; an interest rate floats with pool utilization.
- A **CDP** lets each borrower **mint** the pAsset directly from the protocol. You are not borrowing anyone's deposit; you are your own mini central bank: post collateral ⇒ mint a pAsset ⇒ owe that debt back.

Each position is isolated in a **[trove](/minting/managing-your-trove)** with its own collateral, debt, and collateralization ratio. What connects all users of a given pAsset is only (1) the pAsset and the state of its peg, and (2) system-wide parameters like the [Total Collateralization Ratio and Recovery Mode](/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode). That isolation bounds the blast radius: each pAsset keeps its own debt accounting, so trouble in one pAsset cannot reach the troves or collateral of another.

### Strengths and trade-offs

The CDP model is ~8 years proven. Its strengths and weaknesses are well understood; Polaris's design amplifies the former and dampens the latter.

| Strengths | Trade-offs |
|---|---|
| **Trustless** — logic fully enforced onchain, no intermediaries | **Liquidation risk** — collateral below threshold can be [liquidated](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations) |
| **Transparent** — ratios and liquidation logic auditable onchain | **Volatility** — sharp collateral drops can trigger liquidations |
| **Optionality** — unlock liquidity without selling your assets | **Smart-contract risk** — mitigated by audits, never zero |
| **Debt-asset agnostic** — the minted asset can be anything | **Capital inefficiency** — overcollateralization leaves capital idle |

## What Polaris does differently

### Collateral is pETH, and only pETH

Polaris does not accept wstETH or other staked-ETH tokens: they would be external dependencies that cap scaling. It accepts only **[pETH](/peth/bonding-curve)**, ETH deposited into the bonding curve. This is what dampens the CDP model's capital-inefficiency weakness, because pETH is:

1. an ETH proxy with an **ever-rising floor price**, and
2. **yield-bearing** — collateral pETH also earns from other parts of the system (e.g. ETH released by conversions).

### Autonomous, market-aware interest rates

Setting CDP interest rates is where most designs struggle: Maker still uses governance-set rates (spiky, unpredictable); Liquity v1 had none (sustained supply pressure as T-bill rates rose); Liquity v2 pushes rate-setting onto users.

Polaris sets rates **autonomously, without requiring a direct market-price feed for the pAsset**, by reading signals already inside the system:

- **Redemptions are profitable when pUSD < $1** ⇒ a spike in redemption volume signals a downward depeg ⇒ raise the rate.
- **Minting is profitable when pUSD > $1** ⇒ a spike in minting volume signals an upward depeg ⇒ lower the rate.

This gives the protocol an internal peg-pressure signal, not a substitute for every oracle in the system. Collateral valuation, non-dollar pAsset reference units, stale-price checks, and liquidation math still depend on the final audited pricing paths for those assets. See [Redemptions](/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions) for the mechanism that makes the peg-pressure signal possible.

## Ready to mint

Minting a pAsset means opening a trove. See **[Open a Trove](/minting/open-a-trove)** for the step-by-step, and **[The pAsset Catalog](/minting/passet-catalog)** for what you can mint.

---

<BlogPostCard
  url="https://polarisfinance.io/blog/polaris-mints-anything"
  title="CDPs Mint Dollars. Polaris Mints Anything"
  description="From Maker to Liquity, CDP models shaped DeFi lending. Polaris takes the blueprint further with pETH, self-adjusting rates, and a factory for stablecoins pegged to anything."
  readingTime={10}
  image="https://polarisfinance.io/blog/polaris-mints-anything-cover.png"
/>

---

## Open a Trove

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/minting/open-a-trove

import { Steps } from 'nextra/components'

# Open a Trove

A **trove** is your individual borrowing position: its own collateral, its own debt, its own collateralization ratio. Opening one is how you mint a pAsset: **connect wallet ⇒ acquire pETH ⇒ deposit pETH as collateral ⇒ choose and mint a pAsset ⇒ confirm ⇒ monitor your ICR**. This page walks each step; for the concepts behind it, see [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets) and [Managing Your Trove](/minting/managing-your-trove).

> **Note:** Screenshots and exact UI labels are *forthcoming* and will be added once the app is live. The steps below describe the flow, not the final button copy.

## Before you start

- A wallet with **ETH on the supported network** (network *forthcoming*).
- A target pAsset in mind — see [the catalog](/minting/passet-catalog).

> **Before you act:**
> Opening a trove creates debt. Check the app's displayed minimum ICR, Recovery Mode status, current borrow rate, redemption queue position, and the pETH amount you will receive after bonding-curve fees and slippage. Exact launch parameters are still pending, so use the live app values rather than examples in this guide.

## Pre-launch parameter status

| Parameter | Status | What to use before launch |
|---|---|---|
| Supported network | Pending final launch confirmation | Treat examples as network-agnostic |
| Minimum ICR per pAsset | Pending final parameters | Use the value shown by the app and contracts at launch |
| Recovery Mode trigger | Pending final parameters | Monitor the app's system-health indicator |
| Borrow interest-rate formula and bounds | Pending final parameters | Use the current quoted rate before minting |
| Redemption fee formula | Pending final parameters | Use the pre-confirmation quote for any transaction |
| pETH curve parameters and swap fee | Pending final audited values | Check the bonding-curve quote before acquiring collateral |

## Steps

<Steps>

### Connect your wallet

Open the Polaris app and connect.

### Acquire pETH

Deposit ETH into the [bonding curve](/peth/bonding-curve) to receive pETH. pETH is the *only* collateral Polaris accepts. If you already hold pETH, skip this step.

### Deposit pETH as collateral

Choose how much pETH to lock in the trove. This is your collateral; it stays in the trove until you withdraw it.

### Choose pAsset and amount

Pick the pAsset (pUSD, pGOLD, …) and the amount of debt to take on. The app shows the resulting **Individual Collateralization Ratio (ICR)** — your collateral value divided by your debt value — before you confirm.

### Confirm

Sign the transaction. The minted pAsset arrives in your wallet; the debt is recorded against your trove.

### Monitor your ICR

Your ICR moves with the price of ETH and with any changes you make. Watch it from the trove dashboard and adjust before it gets close to a threshold. See [Managing Your Trove](/minting/managing-your-trove).

</Steps>

## Choosing a safe collateralization buffer

Your ICR is the single number that determines your risk. Two distinct events depend on it:

- **[Liquidation](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations).** If your ICR falls below the minimum, your trove is liquidated and you lose collateral to cover the debt (plus a penalty). A low ICR is most dangerous when ETH drops sharply.
- **[Redemption](/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions).** When the pAsset trades below peg, arbitrageurs redeem against the *lowest*-ICR troves first. Being redeemed against is not a liquidation penalty, but it shrinks your position and your directional exposure without your consent.

A larger buffer above the minimum protects you on both fronts: it keeps you clear of liquidation through volatility, and it keeps you out of the front of the redemption queue. The right buffer depends on how volatile you expect ETH to be and how actively you intend to manage the trove — a position you watch daily can run leaner than one you set and forget.

> **Warning:** There is no margin call and no grace period. If ETH moves against you faster than you top up, liquidation is automatic. Size your buffer for the worst day you are willing to sit through, not the average one.

## Worked example: ICR buffer

The numbers below are illustrative only. They show the math; they are not launch parameters.

Assume the app displays a 120% minimum ICR for a pUSD trove, and you open with $18,000 of pETH collateral against 10,000 pUSD of debt:

| Step | Collateral value | Debt | ICR |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Opening position | $18,000 | 10,000 pUSD | 180% |
| After a 20% collateral-value drop | $14,400 | 10,000 pUSD | 144% |
| After the same drop plus 200 pUSD of accrued interest | $14,400 | 10,200 pUSD | 141% |

That buffer survives the modeled move. If the same user opened at 135% instead, a 20% collateral-value drop would take the trove to about 108%, below the illustrative 120% minimum. The lesson is not that 180% is the right number; it is that your buffer has to cover price moves, interest accrual, gas delays, and the possibility that redemptions target the lowest-ICR troves first.

---

## pAsset Catalog

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/minting/passet-catalog

# pAsset Catalog

A **pAsset** is a synthetic asset minted by Polaris against pETH collateral. Each is overcollateralized, counterparty-free, and redeemable through onchain protocol mechanics under the usual assumptions: functioning contracts, live Ethereum execution, available gas, and valid pricing data. The catalog is open: through [StablecoinOS](#stablecoinos-an-open-catalog), credible teams can add new pAssets, and every one of them shares the same pETH collateral and bonding curve.

## The catalog

| pAsset | Tracks | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **pUSD** | US dollar | The flagship pAsset. Counterparty-free dollar with yield that scales with protocol activity. See [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets). |
| **pETH** | ETH | *Special.* Not minted from a trove: it is the collateral itself, produced by the [bonding curve](/peth/bonding-curve) with an ever-rising floor price. Every other pAsset is backed by it. |
| **pGOLD** | 1 ounce of gold | Decentralized gold exposure, backed by pETH rather than bullion. See the comparison below. |
| **pCHF** | Swiss franc | A non-USD fiat peg, demonstrating that the model is debt-asset agnostic across currencies. |
| **pBigMac** | The [Big Mac Index](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_Mac_Index) | A novelty pAsset tracking the price of a Big Mac, proof that Polaris can mint anything with a price feed and a flexible enough fee flow. |

## pGOLD vs centralized gold (XAUT/PAXG)

pGOLD and the incumbent gold tokens both give you gold-price exposure, but they are structurally different products.

| Property | pGOLD | XAUT / PAXG |
|---|---|---|
| Collateral | pETH, fully onchain | Physical gold in custody |
| Redemption | Permissionless, no protocol-level minimum published yet | Issuer-gated; account requirements and redemption minimums depend on the issuer's current terms |
| KYC | None | Required to redeem |
| Physical custody | None — nothing to store or insure | Vaulted bullion, with custody risk |
| Counterparty | Polaris protocol only | Tether / Paxos as issuer |

The trade-off is honest: pGOLD inherits ETH-collateral and smart-contract risk where XAUT/PAXG inherit custodial and issuer risk. What pGOLD removes is the *permissioned* layer: there is no counterparty that can freeze, blacklist, or gate your redemption.

## StablecoinOS: an open catalog

The CDP model is **debt-asset agnostic**: it can mint a synthetic pegged to many assets that have robust reference pricing and risk parameters. Most protocols ship one stablecoin and stop. Polaris is built as a *factory* of CDP instances under **StablecoinOS**: one CDP per pAsset, all sharing the same pETH collateral pool and the same bonding curve.

That shared collateral does double duty. It lets credible teams add a new pAsset (pEUR, pSilver, or something stranger) without bootstrapping a fresh collateral base. And because each instance has its own debt asset, a violent day in one market (a gold spike, say) drives liquidations on that instance while the others barely move. The pETH collateral is mutualized; the risk is not.

The result is a catalog that grows without diluting the safety of what already exists.

---

<BlogPostCard
  url="https://polarisfinance.io/blog/pGOLD-finishing-what-digixdao-started"
  title="pGOLD: Finishing What DigixDAO Started"
  description="How pGOLD breaks the XAUT/PAXG duopoly without touching a single gold bar."
  readingTime={7}
  image="https://polarisfinance.io/blog/pgold-finishing-what-digixdao-started-cover.png"
/>

---

## Bonding Curve

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/peth/bonding-curve

# Bonding Curve

The bonding curve is the foundation of Polaris. From this one primitive comes pETH, its rising floor, non-liquidatable ETH loans, and value capture on every interaction.

## What is a bonding curve?

A two-way bonding curve lets you swap, just like a regular AMM pool, but with one key difference: **it needs no liquidity providers.** Instead, it enforces a mathematical relationship between a token's price and its supply:

- Demand rises, tokens are minted, price increases.
- Tokens are burned/sold, price decreases.

Buying adds ETH to the reserve and **mints** new pETH. Selling withdraws from the reserve and **burns** pETH. The result is contract-native liquidity with no external liquidity provider, subject to Ethereum liveness, available gas, MEV, slippage, and the bonding-curve contracts functioning as designed. You have used bonding curves without knowing it: every AMM is one (Uniswap, Curve), and Pump.fun is a well-known example.

Two things make pETH's curve different from Pump.fun's:

1. **Purpose**: Pump.fun raises capital to seed liquidity; Polaris uses the curve to *back* pETH permanently.
2. **Temporality**: Pump.fun's curve terminates at "graduation"; the pETH curve is designed to keep running.

## What's unique about pETH's curve

pETH's curve is **two-way** (ETH ⇒ pETH and pETH ⇒ ETH) and it **enforces a rising price floor** for pETH, measured in ETH, under the curve's audited invariants. The floor is designed to move up through two mechanisms:

- A fee is charged on each swap, in pETH, and **burned** ⇒ the floor rises with every interaction.
- The [conversion auction](/polar/conversion-auctions) mints POLAR by **burning pETH** ⇒ the floor rises with POLAR minting.

![pETH bonding curve: ETH deposits mint pETH along a power-law curve with a rising floor price](/infographics/polaris-bonding-curve.svg)

A rising floor is more than a safety hatch for holders. It lets the **whole protocol capture value with each interaction**, and defines a reserve-backed exit path while the contracts are live, Ethereum is processing transactions, and users can pay execution costs.

### The maths

The curve follows the formula:

$$
P = \alpha \cdot Q^{\beta}
$$

- **α**: a scaling parameter of little importance.
- **Q**: the supply of pETH.
- **β**: the power-law exponent that determines the curve's behaviour.

As supply `Q` increases, the price of pETH (in ETH) increases. **β** sets how tightly pETH tracks ETH: a large β decouples pETH and makes it volatile; a small β couples it closely. Polaris will use **β around 0.3**: close coupling, with enough room for interesting dynamics.

> *The exact final β is disclosed closer to launch.*

## pETH beyond the CDP

pETH is a tailor-made collateral for Polaris CDPs, but it has a life of its own.

### ETH loans (pETH loops)

You can supply pETH back into the bonding curve and **borrow the floor-price value in ETH** from the reserve. These loans are:

- **Non-liquidatable by price movement**: there is no collateral-price liquidation trigger.
- **Withdrawable through repayment**: repay the ETH owed and reclaim your pETH while the contracts and Ethereum are live.
- **Recorded onchain as an NFT.**

Because the loan is sized to the floor price, it is designed to remain solvent for the protocol under the floor-price invariant: a borrower who never repays has effectively sold pETH to the curve at floor price, a good deal for the curve if pETH trades at a premium to its floor. Looping pETH is a way to take **leveraged ETH-aligned exposure to Polaris's growth without touching anything but ETH.**

### DeFi composability

pETH's rising floor and eventual deep ETH backing could make it a prime asset *outside* Polaris too. A pETH/ETH market on Aave, Morpho, or Euler is a natural fit, much like stETH is used across DeFi today.

## pETH vs stETH

stETH is a prime asset, but it does not have contract-native liquidity from its own reserve. Native withdrawals depend on the validator exit queue and can vary; DEX liquidity is deep, but depends on liquidity providers who can stop supplying in a panic.

| | **pETH** | **stETH** |
|---|---|---|
| Liquidity | Bonding-curve reserve, subject to contract and chain liveness | Deep, but LP-dependent |
| Withdrawal | Onchain sell path, with gas/slippage/MEV | Validator-exit queue; timing varies |
| Price floor | Contract-enforced under audited invariants | None |
| Main risk | Price risk vs ETH, bounded by the floor under the curve assumptions | LP/liquidity risk |

> stETH is a prime asset, don't get us wrong. It simply does not provide this kind of reserve-backed onchain sell path.

## Risk overview

pETH carries three main risks:

1. **Technical risk**: the bonding curve is a smart contract; technical risk is unavoidable, mitigated through audits and modeling.
2. **Execution risk**: using the curve still requires Ethereum liveness, available gas, and transactions that can tolerate slippage and MEV.
3. **Price risk**: pETH is volatile against ETH. Your exposure depends on your entry price relative to the floor, i.e. the [floor ratio](/peth/floor-price). Entering at a 90% floor ratio (10% curve-implied drawdown before execution costs) is very different from a 66% floor ratio (33% curve-implied drawdown before execution costs).

Liquidity-provider withdrawal risk is removed by the curve design. It is not the same as saying execution is riskless: the curve provides a contract-defined buy/sell path and floor while the relevant invariants hold.

---

<BlogPostCard
  url="https://polarisfinance.io/blog/bonding-curve"
  title="The Bonding Curve: Polaris' Secret Weapon"
  description="How a single mechanism enables pETH's rising floor, guaranteed liquidity, and protocol-wide value capture."
  readingTime={10}
  image="https://polarisfinance.io/blog/bonding-curve-cover.png"
/>

---

## Floor Price

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/peth/floor-price

# Floor Price

## What it is

The **floor price** is the mathematically enforced minimum price of pETH, expressed in ETH, under the bonding-curve invariants. It is the curve-defined price for the *last person out* if everyone exited the bonding curve at once — assuming the contracts function as designed, Ethereum is live, and users can pay execution costs. Because most exiters get a better curve price than that, it is a protocol worst case, not an average.

The floor is not a promise or a target defended by a treasury. It is a direct consequence of the bonding curve's reserves and supply, enforced by immutable code and dependent on the audited invariants holding.

## Why it only rises

Two mechanisms are designed to push the floor up, with no normal protocol action designed to push it down:

- **Swap fee burns.** Every swap on the [bonding curve](/peth/bonding-curve) charges a fee in pETH, which is burned. Burning supply against an unchanged-or-growing ETH reserve raises the floor.
- **Conversion auctions.** Minting POLAR requires [burning pETH](/polar/conversion-auctions), which again reduces supply and lifts the floor.

The result is a staircase under normal operation: the market price of pETH fluctuates above the floor, while the floor itself steps upward over time.

## The floor ratio

The **floor ratio** expresses the floor as a percentage of the current market price:

$$
\text{Floor ratio} = \frac{\text{Floor price}}{\text{Market price}} \times 100
$$

It tells you the curve-implied drawdown to the floor before gas, slippage, MEV, and smart-contract or chain-liveness risk. A floor ratio of 90% means 10% downside to the floor in ETH terms under those assumptions; a floor ratio of 66% means 33% downside to the floor.

| Floor ratio | Curve-implied drawdown to floor |
|---|---|
| 90% | 10% |
| 66% | 33% |
| 55% | 45% |

The floor ratio is itself volatile, driven by market sentiment and activity:

- In phases of **wild growth**, the market price runs far ahead of the floor, so the floor ratio can fall, sometimes below 50%.
- As the market price **normalizes**, the floor catches up and the ratio rises, until the limited remaining downside makes pETH attractive again.

## Assumptions behind the floor

| Assumption | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Bonding-curve contracts function as audited | The floor is a contract invariant, not an offchain guarantee |
| Ethereum remains live | Exiting requires a transaction that can be included |
| Users can pay gas | High gas can make small exits uneconomic |
| Slippage and MEV are tolerated or bounded | The quoted curve price and realized execution can differ |
| The relevant invariants are not changed or broken | The floor depends on reserves, supply accounting, and burn/mint rules |

> Your risk in pETH is decided at entry: it is the spread between your entry price and the floor (the floor ratio), plus execution and technical risk. Under the intended curve invariant, the floor is designed to move up rather than down.

---

<BlogPostCard
  url="https://polarisfinance.io/blog/bonding-curve"
  title="The Bonding Curve: Polaris' Secret Weapon"
  description="How a single mechanism enables pETH's rising floor, guaranteed liquidity, and protocol-wide value capture."
  readingTime={10}
  image="https://polarisfinance.io/blog/bonding-curve-cover.png"
/>

---

## Deposit to the Stability Pool

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/yield/deposit-to-stability-pool

import { Steps } from 'nextra/components'

# Deposit to the Stability Pool

This page walks through depositing a pAsset (pUSD in the examples) into the [Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool), claiming your rewards, and withdrawing. Read [The Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool) first if you have not — it explains what you earn and the risk you take on.

> **Note:**
> The product interface is not yet public. Screenshots and the exact UI flow are forthcoming; the steps below describe the intended flow and will be updated with images at launch.

## Before you start

You need:

- A pAsset to deposit — most commonly **pUSD**. [Mint pUSD](/minting/minting-passets) first if you hold none.
- ETH for gas.
- An understanding that liquidation gains arrive in **pETH**, and the [risk](/yield/stability-pool#the-honest-risk) of a severe crash. If you want pure pUSD exposure, use a wrapper vault — see [Where the Yield Comes From](/yield/yield-sources).

> **Before you act:**
> Check the pool's current size, your projected share, accrued reward tokens, withdrawal conditions, and whether you are comfortable receiving pETH during volatile liquidations. If you plan to convert pETH rewards back to pAsset, account for gas, slippage, MEV, and wrapper-vault availability.

> **Warning:**
> A Stability Pool deposit is at risk of loss. Liquidations convert part of your deposited pAsset into pETH, and in a severe or flash crash the pETH you receive can be worth **less** than the pAsset burned — your principal is not protected. Size your deposit accordingly and read the [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure).

## Deposit

<Steps>

### Open the pool

Open the Polaris app and go to the **Stability Pool** for your pAsset (e.g. the pUSD pool).

### Enter an amount

Enter the amount of pUSD to deposit.

### Approve the spend

**Approve** the pUSD spend, if prompted (a one-time token approval).

### Confirm the deposit

Confirm the **deposit** transaction in your wallet.

### Position is active

Your share of the pool is now active. It begins accruing borrower-interest yield immediately and is eligible for liquidation gains from the next liquidation onward.

</Steps>

## Claim rewards

Your two reward streams behave differently:

- **Interest share (pUSD)** accrues continuously to your position.
- **Liquidation gains (pETH)** accumulate to your position each time a liquidation is processed.

To realize them:

<Steps>

### Open your position

In the Stability Pool view, open your position to see **claimable pUSD** and **claimable pETH**.

### Claim or compound

Select **Claim** to withdraw rewards to your wallet, or compound them back into the pool where supported.

### Confirm

Confirm the transaction in your wallet.

</Steps>

## Withdraw

<Steps>

### Choose Withdraw

In the Stability Pool view, choose **Withdraw**.

### Enter an amount

Enter the amount of pUSD to remove, or select the full balance.

### Confirm

Confirm the transaction in your wallet.

</Steps>

Any unclaimed rewards are settled when you withdraw. Note that your deposited pUSD balance fluctuates as liquidations convert part of it into pETH — what you withdraw reflects the pool's current state, not a fixed principal.

## Next steps

- [The Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool) — the concept, in full.
- [Where the Yield Comes From](/yield/yield-sources) — every source feeding your yield.

---

## Stability Pool

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/yield/stability-pool

# Stability Pool

> **Before you deposit:**
> A Stability Pool deposit is not a fixed pAsset savings balance. Liquidations can burn part of your deposited pAsset and replace it with pETH rewards. Check the current pool size, claimable rewards, pETH price exposure, withdrawal conditions, and whether wrapper vaults are available before depositing.

## What it is

The **Stability Pool** is a reserve of pAssets — pUSD, for example — that depositors supply to the protocol. It exists to backstop [liquidations](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations): it holds pUSD on standby so that, when a [trove](/minting/managing-your-trove) falls below its minimum collateralization ratio, the protocol can close out that debt through the liquidation path.

Each [pAsset](/minting/passet-catalog) has its own Stability Pool. A pUSD pool backstops pUSD troves, a pGOLD pool backstops pGOLD troves, and so on across the [StablecoinOS](/core-concepts). One pool per instance bounds the blast radius: a depositor is only ever exposed to liquidations of the pAsset they deposited, never to losses in another pAsset's pool.

## How it processes liquidations

Most CDP designs require a liquidator to bring fresh capital to close a bad position. Polaris does not. The capital is already pooled.

When a trove is liquidated:

1. The pool **burns** an amount of its pUSD equal to the liquidated debt.
2. The liquidated trove's **pETH collateral is paid out** to depositors, pro rata to their share of the pool.

Because liquidations carry a penalty, the pETH paid out is worth *more* than the pUSD burned. In effect, a depositor's pUSD is converted into pETH at a discount to market — typically a net gain.

> **Note:**
> Liquidation gains arrive as **pETH**, not pUSD. If you want pure pUSD exposure, wrapper vaults that convert that pETH back to pUSD on your behalf will be available at or shortly after launch. See [Where the Yield Comes From](/yield/yield-sources).

## Why it is the primary yield venue

Depositing in the Stability Pool earns two uncorrelated streams:

| Stream | Paid in | Character |
|---|---|---|
| **Share of borrower interest** | pUSD | Regular, continuous, non-spiky |
| **Liquidation gains** | pETH | Event-driven, spiky, arrives with volatility |

The interest share is the steady base; liquidation gains spike precisely when markets are turbulent. Together they make the Stability Pool the protocol's **primary yield venue** for pAssets — and the foundation other yield products are built on top of. The share of borrower interest routed to depositors is [stewarded](/stewardship/stewardship) by vePOLAR within hardcoded bounds (a floor, to keep the pool funded for liquidations; a ceiling, to keep pUSD liquid across DeFi); exact values are finalized closer to launch.

## Pre-launch parameter status

| Parameter | Status | Reader impact |
|---|---|---|
| Borrower-interest share to Stability Pool | Pending final stewarded bounds and launch value | Determines steady pAsset-denominated yield |
| Liquidation penalty / depositor discount | Pending final values | Determines expected pETH upside on healthy liquidations |
| Reward claim and compounding behavior | Pending final UI and contract details | Determines whether rewards accrue separately or auto-settle |
| Wrapper vault availability | Expected at or shortly after launch; final availability pending | Determines whether users can automate pETH-to-pAsset conversion |
| Withdrawal constraints | Pending final implementation | Determines how quickly and under what conditions deposits can exit |
| pETH pricing and execution assumptions | Pending final audited paths | Determines realized value when rewards are sold or wrapped |

## Worked example: Stability Pool P&L

Suppose you deposit 10,000 pUSD into a 1,000,000 pUSD Stability Pool. You own 1% of the pool. A liquidation burns 100,000 pUSD from the pool and pays out pETH that is worth $110,000 at execution.

| Item | Pool-level amount | Your 1% share |
|---|---:|---:|
| pUSD burned | 100,000 pUSD | 1,000 pUSD |
| pETH received at execution value | $110,000 | $1,100 |
| Gross liquidation gain before price movement | $10,000 | $100 |

That is the healthy-liquidation case: part of your pUSD position converted into discounted pETH. If pETH falls 15% before you sell or wrap it, your $1,100 of pETH becomes $935, turning that event into a $65 loss versus the 1,000 pUSD burned. Interest income and later liquidations can offset losses, but they do not remove pETH price risk.

## The honest risk

Depositing is not free of risk.

> **Warning:**
> In a severe or flash crash, a trove can fall below 100% collateralization before liquidation completes. If that happens, the pETH you receive can be worth **less** than the pUSD you contributed. The liquidation penalty makes a net gain the normal case, not a guaranteed one. Size your deposit accordingly, and see the [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure).

Your liquidation gains are also denominated in pETH, which is [volatile against ETH](/peth/floor-price). The wrapper vaults above mitigate that exposure, but at the base layer you are accepting pETH.

## Next steps
