The flagship pAsset, designed to track the US dollar and be backed by pETH. It sits in the same counterparty-free design family as systems like LUSD and BOLD, while adding Polaris's pETH collateral and yield mechanics. See [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets).

**What is pETH?**
ETH deposited into a two-way [bonding curve](/peth/bonding-curve). It is the only collateral Polaris accepts, is yield-bearing, and has a [floor price](/peth/floor-price) that only rises.

**What is POLAR?**
The stewardship token and the protocol's outward-facing growth lever. After the initial distribution, new POLAR can be minted *only* by burning pETH in a [conversion auction](/polar/conversion-auctions). See [The POLAR Token](/polar/polar-token).

**Which chain does Polaris run on?**
Polaris is designed around Ethereum-native collateral and Ethereum security assumptions. The exact supported network and chain ID are still forthcoming and will be published on [Launch & Verification Readiness](/resources/launch-readiness) and [Contracts & Addresses](/resources/contracts) at launch.

**Where is the official app?**
The official app URL is not published in these docs yet. Until it appears on [Launch & Verification Readiness](/resources/launch-readiness), treat any "Polaris app" frontend as unverified.

## Minting and troves

**What is a trove?**
Your individual borrowing position in a Polaris CDP, isolated with its own collateral, debt, and collateralization ratio. Polaris uses *trove*, never "vault". See [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets).

**How do I mint a pAsset?**
You open a trove: post pETH as collateral and mint the pAsset directly from the protocol, owing that debt back. You are your own mini central bank, not borrowing another user's funds. See [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets).

**Why does Polaris accept only pETH as collateral?**
External collateral like staked-ETH tokens would be a dependency that caps scaling and reintroduces counterparty risk. pETH is an ETH proxy with a rising floor that is also yield-bearing, which dampens the CDP model's capital-inefficiency weakness. See [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets).

**How are interest rates set?**
Autonomously, with no governance vote and no price oracle for the peg. Redemption volume (profitable below peg) and minting volume (profitable above peg) are read as onchain signals of where the peg sits, and rates adjust accordingly. See [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets).

**Can my trove be liquidated?**
Yes. If your [ICR](/resources/glossary) falls below the minimum, the trove is [liquidated](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations) and the [Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool) absorbs its debt. Keep your ratio above the threshold, especially in volatile markets.

## pETH

**Is pETH the same as ETH?**
No. pETH is ETH inside the bonding curve. It is volatile against ETH but bounded below by its [floor price](/peth/floor-price), and it earns yield. Think of it as stETH with a different set of trade-offs: guaranteed liquidity and instant withdrawal, but price volatility against ETH.

**What is the floor price, and why does it only go up?**
The floor is the mathematically enforced minimum price of pETH in ETH terms: the price guaranteed to the last person out. It rises because swap fees are burned and conversion auctions burn pETH, and nothing pushes it back down. See [The Floor Price](/peth/floor-price).

## Yield

**Where does the yield come from?**
From onchain activity, not offchain T-bills: CDP interest, bonding-curve swap fees, conversion gains, and PolarEX swap fees, all of which grow with usage. See [The Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool).

**How do I earn yield?**
The primary venue is depositing pAssets into the [Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool). Locking POLAR as [vePOLAR](/stewardship/vepolar) also earns a share of interest and conversion ETH yield.

## Stewardship

**Is there governance?**
No unbounded governance. The core is immutable; [vePOLAR](/stewardship/vepolar) holders steward only quantitative parameters within hardcoded ranges. We call it stewardship because stewards cannot alter the core; they cannot govern it. See [Stewardship, not Governance](/stewardship/stewardship).

**What can stewards actually change?**
Quantitative parameters within fixed bounds: CDP licensing and parametrization, Fee Router splits, Flow whitelist and weight settings, conversion parameters, and onboarding. They cannot exceed any hardcoded range, and they cannot touch the core logic. See [Stewardship](/stewardship/stewardship) and [Flows](/stewardship/flows).

## Security

**Can I lose funds?**
Yes. Polaris carries smart-contract risk, collateral price risk, liquidation risk, and Stability Pool first-loss risk, among others. Trustlessness means no one can step in to make you whole. Read the full [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure) before using the protocol.

**Is Polaris audited?**
Final audit reports are not published in these docs yet. Until reports appear on [Audits & Security](/resources/audits-security), treat the protocol as unaudited.

**Where are contract addresses listed?**
Canonical addresses are not published yet. Once deployed, addresses will be listed with network, chain ID, and source-verification links on [Contracts & Addresses](/resources/contracts). Any address found elsewhere before then is unverified.

**Can my pAssets be frozen or blacklisted?**
The intended design is immutable and admin-key-free, with no freeze function and no blacklist, by anyone, including the team. Verify the final deployed contracts on [Contracts & Addresses](/resources/contracts) once addresses are published.

---

Still stuck? Look up any term in the [Glossary](/resources/glossary), or read the long-form essays on the [Polaris blog](https://polarisfinance.io/blog).

---

## Glossary

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/resources/glossary

# Glossary

Every key Polaris term, alphabetized. Each entry is one or two sentences and links to its full treatment where one exists. For the narrative map of how the pieces fit together, read [Core Concepts](/core-concepts).

## B

**Bonding curve**
The two-way curve that backs [pETH](/peth/bonding-curve). It enforces a mathematical relationship between pETH supply and price (minting raises it, burning lowers it), providing instant, guaranteed liquidity with no liquidity providers, and an ever-rising [floor price](/peth/floor-price). See [The Bonding Curve](/peth/bonding-curve).

## C

**CDP (Collateralized Debt Position)**
The mechanism behind minting: post collateral, mint a pAsset directly from the protocol rather than borrowing another user's funds. Each position is an isolated [trove](#t). See [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets).

**Conversion auction**
The mechanism that connects pETH and POLAR: it burns [pETH](/peth/bonding-curve) to mint new [POLAR](/polar/polar-token). It is the most protocol-synergetic action possible: every conversion raises the floor and releases ETH yield. See [Conversion Auctions](/polar/conversion-auctions).

**Converter rebate**
A stewardship-controlled percentage of ETH returned to the converter in a [conversion auction](/polar/conversion-auctions). It is the dial between conversion volume and system yield.

## F

**Fee Router**
Receives pETH from conversions and swap fees, buffers it, and distributes the yield to troves and [vePOLAR](/stewardship/vepolar) lockers. See [Stewardship](/stewardship/stewardship).

**Floor price**
The mathematically enforced minimum price of [pETH](/peth/bonding-curve), in ETH terms. It only ever rises. It is the price guaranteed to the *last person out* if everyone exited at once; most exiters get a better price. See [The Floor Price](/peth/floor-price).

**Floor ratio**
The floor price divided by the market price, expressed as a percentage. It tells you your maximum drawdown: a 90% floor ratio means at most 10% downside in ETH terms. See [The Floor Price](/peth/floor-price).

**Flows**
The internal value movements of the protocol (fees, conversions, yield distribution), routed by the [Fee Router](#f). Inward-facing flows sustain the core; outward-facing flows grow the ecosystem (see *Inward-facing / Outward-facing*).

## I

**ICR (Individual Collateralization Ratio)**
The collateralization ratio of a single [trove](#t): the value of its pETH collateral divided by its pAsset debt. If a trove's ICR falls below the minimum, the trove can be [liquidated](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations).

**Inward-facing / Outward-facing**
The design split that decides which token handles what. *Inward-facing* flows (interest rates, floor growth, Stability Pool incentivization, liquidations) are handled by pETH and the pAssets. *Outward-facing* growth (external liquidity, Flow onboarding and weights, ecosystem incentives) is handled by [POLAR](/polar/polar-token).

## L

**Liquidation**
The process that closes an undercollateralized [trove](#t): when a trove's [ICR](#i) drops below the minimum, the [Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool) absorbs its debt and receives its collateral. See [Liquidations](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations).

## M

**Minting (mechanism)**
The inverse of [redemption](#r): when a pAsset trades *above* peg (e.g. pUSD > $1), arbitrageurs add pETH to troves to mint the pAsset and sell it, pushing the price back down. This restores the ceiling and is one of the onchain signals used to set interest rates. See [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets).

**Minting (verb)**
Creating a pAsset by opening a [trove](#t) and drawing pAsset debt against pETH collateral. Polaris *mints* pAssets, not "issues" or "creates" them.

## O

**Oracle**
An onchain feed Polaris is expected to use to price ETH collateral. Peg awareness itself needs no oracle: redemption and minting volume serve as an internal price signal for each pAsset. The final provider, feed address, supported network, and chain ID are forthcoming. See [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets) and [Contracts & Addresses](/resources/contracts).

## P

**pAsset**
A Polaris synthetic asset — pUSD, pETH, pGOLD, pCHF, and beyond. Each is overcollateralized by pETH and counterparty-free. See [The pAsset Catalog](/minting/passet-catalog).

**pBigMac**
A novelty pAsset that demonstrates the "mint anything" thesis: if a price feed exists, Polaris can mint a pAsset that tracks it.

**pCHF**
The Polaris synthetic Swiss franc — a pAsset pegged to CHF.

**pETH**
ETH deposited into the two-way [bonding curve](/peth/bonding-curve). It is the *only* collateral Polaris accepts, is yield-bearing, and carries an ever-rising [floor price](/peth/floor-price). Volatile against ETH, but bounded below by the floor. See [The Bonding Curve](/peth/bonding-curve).

**pGOLD**
The Polaris synthetic gold pAsset, designed to track gold without issuer custody or KYC at the base protocol layer. See [The pAsset Catalog](/minting/passet-catalog).

**Pathfinders**
Core believers, not just early followers. Access is earned through merit (explanatory threads, deep research, finding Easter eggs) and is rewarded with early alphas, testnet access, and dev-stage updates.

**Polar Council**
A treasury-funded program (8.2% of POLAR supply) for onboarding and ecosystem contributors, similar in spirit to veAERO's Flight School.

**POLAR**
The protocol's stewardship token, and its outward-facing growth lever. After the initial distribution, new POLAR can be minted *only* by burning pETH in a [conversion auction](/polar/conversion-auctions). See [The POLAR Token](/polar/polar-token).

**PolarEX**
Polaris' integrated DEX. Its swap fees are one of the system's yield sources.

**pUSD**
The flagship pAsset, designed to track the US dollar and be backed by pETH collateral. It is intended to be counterparty-free and fully onchain. See [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets).

## R

**Recovery Mode**
A protocol-wide protective state triggered when the [TCR](#t) falls below a threshold. While active, the system tightens requirements to restore collateralization. See [Recovery Mode](/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode).

**Redemption**
Exchanging a pAsset for its underlying collateral at face value (minus a fee) when the pAsset trades *below* peg. Redemptions restore the floor and are one of the onchain signals used to set interest rates. See [Redemptions](/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions).

## S

**Spike-and-decay**
The pricing model for [conversion auctions](/polar/conversion-auctions): the conversion price continuously decays until a conversion *spikes* it upward, proportionally to the POLAR supply that conversion inflated.

**Stability Pool**
A reserve of pAssets that absorbs the debt of [liquidated](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations) troves and is the protocol's primary venue for earning yield. See [The Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool).

**StablecoinOS**
The CDP-factory layer: many distinct pAssets, each issued by its own CDP instance, all sharing the same pETH collateral and bonding curve. See [Core Concepts](/core-concepts).

**Stewardship**
The bounded role [vePOLAR](/stewardship/vepolar) holders play: tuning *quantitative parameters within hardcoded bounds*. The immutable core cannot be changed; stewards steward, they do not govern. See [Stewardship, not Governance](/stewardship/stewardship).

## T

**TCR (Total Collateralization Ratio)**
The system-wide health metric: total collateral value divided by total pAsset debt. Dropping below a threshold triggers [Recovery Mode](/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode).

**Trove**
An individual borrowing position in a Polaris CDP. Each trove is isolated, with its own collateral, debt, and [ICR](#i). Polaris uses *trove*, never "vault" or "position". See [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets).

## V

**vePOLAR**
Vote-escrowed POLAR — POLAR locked for [stewardship](/stewardship/stewardship) rights and a share of protocol yield. vePOLAR *stewards* parameters within hardcoded bounds; it does not govern. See [vePOLAR](/stewardship/vepolar).

---

Missing a term? The narrative version of every concept lives on its dedicated page, starting from [Core Concepts](/core-concepts), and the long-form essays are on the [Polaris blog](https://polarisfinance.io/blog).

---

## Risk Disclosure

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/resources/risk-disclosure

# Risk Disclosure

Polaris is trustless, immutable infrastructure. That is a design choice with real costs, and we disclose them before we claim the benefits. Every risk below is paired with the mechanism that mitigates it (*mitigates*, not eliminates). Read this page in full before you mint, deposit, or lock anything.

> **Note:** Nothing here is financial advice. Polaris is pre-launch: the official app URL, supported network and chain ID, contract addresses, final audit reports, and disclosure program are not published in these docs yet. Trustlessness means the protocol will not, and cannot, step in to make you whole. You are responsible for understanding the position you take.

## Smart-contract risk

Polaris lives entirely onchain. Its bonding curve, CDPs, Stability Pool, and conversion auctions are smart contracts, and any smart contract can contain bugs.

**Mitigation.** The intended core is immutable and admin-key-free, which removes upgrade and governance-key attack surface but raises the stakes on getting the code right. Final audit reports are not published in these docs yet; until reports appear on [Audits & Security](/resources/audits-security), treat the protocol as unaudited. Immutability is a double-edged sword: it cannot be tampered with, and it cannot be patched. Residual risk is never zero.

## Collateral and price risk

The only collateral is [pETH](/peth/bonding-curve), which is **volatile against ETH**. pETH trades at a varying premium above its [floor price](/peth/floor-price), and that premium can compress sharply in a drawdown.

**Mitigation.** The [floor ratio](/peth/floor-price) bounds your maximum downside in ETH terms: a 90% floor ratio means at most 10% drawdown, a 67% floor ratio at most 33%. Your risk is decided at *entry*: the spread between your entry price and the floor. The floor only ever rises; it cannot retreat.

> **Warning:** A high floor ratio is not a guarantee against ETH's own volatility. pETH protects you in *ETH terms*. If ETH itself falls against the dollar, your pETH falls with it.

## Oracle risk

Pricing pETH collateral for liquidation purposes requires an ETH price feed. A stale, manipulated, or failed oracle could misprice collateral and trigger wrong liquidations, or fail to trigger correct ones.

**Mitigation.** Peg awareness needs no oracle at all: redemption and minting volume act as the protocol's internal price signal for each pAsset (see [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets)). The oracle dependency is intended to be confined to pricing the *collateral*, ETH. The final provider, feed address, supported network, and chain ID are forthcoming; do not treat any oracle address as official until it is listed in [Contracts & Addresses](/resources/contracts).

## Peg and depeg risk

A pAsset can trade away from its peg, below it in a panic or above it in a demand spike. Depegs can be temporary or, under severe stress, sustained.

**Mitigation.** Two opposing arbitrage mechanisms defend the peg structurally:

- **[Redemptions](/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions)** make it profitable to buy the pAsset below peg and redeem it for collateral, pushing the price up toward $1.
- **Minting** makes it profitable to mint and sell the pAsset above peg, pushing the price back down.

Because both are profitable for arbitrageurs the moment the peg slips, they activate without anyone's permission. pUSD is designed as a counterparty-free stablecoin in the same trust-minimized family as LUSD and BOLD, so the peg is not propped up by a redeemable offchain reserve; it is held by onchain incentives.

## Liquidation risk (borrowers)

If your [trove](/minting/minting-passets)'s [Individual Collateralization Ratio (ICR)](/resources/glossary) falls below the minimum, the trove is [liquidated](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations): you lose your collateral to cover the debt.

**Mitigation.** Liquidation logic is transparent and auditable onchain, with no discretionary calls. You control your own ICR: post more collateral, or repay debt, to stay clear of the threshold. pETH's rising floor and yield-bearing nature dampen, but do not remove, the volatility that causes liquidations. Monitor your trove actively, especially in volatile markets.

## Stability Pool first-loss risk (depositors)

Depositing pAssets into the [Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool) is the primary way to earn yield, but the Pool is the **first-loss buffer** for the system. When a trove is liquidated, your deposited pAssets are burned to cancel its debt, and you receive the liquidated pETH collateral in exchange.

**Mitigation.** Liquidations are designed to occur while the trove is still overcollateralized, so depositors typically receive collateral worth *more* than the pAssets they gave up, and that surplus is the source of the Pool's yield. The risk is the opposite case: a liquidation processed below the value of the burned deposit, or a sharp collapse in the value of the received collateral. You are taking on liquidation processing risk in exchange for yield; size your deposit accordingly.

## Recovery Mode

When the system-wide reserve backing relative to total debt (the [reserve-to-debt ratio, shown as the TCR](/resources/glossary)) drops below a threshold, the protocol enters [Recovery Mode](/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode), a defensive state with tighter requirements.

**Mitigation.** Recovery Mode is a feature, not a failure: it raises the bar for the whole system to rebuild collateralization quickly. The cost is borne by borrowers: while the system is in Recovery Mode you cannot withdraw collateral or take on more debt unless you clear a higher minimum ratio and do not lower your ICR (pure collateral top-ups and repayments stay allowed). Liquidation eligibility itself does not change; it stays at the normal minimum in every mode. Keep your ICR comfortably above the minimum so a stress event does not box in your ability to act.

## Stewardship and economic risk

Stewards control quantitative parameters: the interest splits, conversion-price parameters, and the [converter rebate](/resources/glossary). Parameter changes can affect your yield and the economics of your position.

**Mitigation.** Stewardship is *constrained*: many economic parameters move only within hardcoded ranges (for example the converter rebate and the interest-split floors), and the core logic cannot be touched at all (see [Stewardship, not Governance](/stewardship/stewardship)). Some conversion-price parameters are not yet bounded in the current prototype; instead, conversion issuance is rate-limited by its spike-and-decay pricing, so each conversion raises the price in proportion to the POLAR it mints and large or rapid minting throttles itself. The risk that remains is ordinary parameter risk, not the unbounded-governance risk that has denatured other protocols. Final per-parameter bounds are pending audited deployment.

## MEV risk

Redemptions, liquidations, and [conversion auctions](/polar/conversion-auctions) are profitable, permissionless actions, so they attract MEV searchers and arbitrage bots. Ordinary users competing by hand will usually lose the race to automation.

**Mitigation.** This is by design: MEV competition is what keeps the peg tight and the floor rising, because searchers do the protocol's stabilizing work for profit. As a user, do not expect to win arbitrage races manually; interact with the protocol for the position you want (a trove, a Stability Pool deposit, a pETH hold), not to front-run the bots.

## Dependency risk (Ethereum)

Polaris is designed around Ethereum-native collateral and Ethereum security assumptions. The exact supported network and chain ID are forthcoming; once deployed, Polaris inherits the risks of that network: consensus failures, severe congestion, or a contentious fork.

**Mitigation.** This dependency is deliberate and unhedged: the only counterparties Polaris is intended to ask you to trust are immutable code and the supported Ethereum network itself. We do not spread across chains to dilute that exposure, because doing so would reintroduce the bridge and external-dependency risks Polaris exists to avoid. Verify the supported network and chain ID on [Launch & Verification Readiness](/resources/launch-readiness) before signing.

## Frontend and phishing risk

Before launch, fake frontends, fake contract addresses, and fake support channels can appear before users know what the official ones look like. A malicious frontend can ask you to approve tokens, sign dangerous messages, or connect to the wrong network.

**Mitigation.** Do not use any app URL, network, chain ID, contract address, audit claim, or bug-bounty link unless it is listed in these docs. Never enter a seed phrase, private key, or recovery words into any Polaris-related site. The current status checklist lives on [Launch & Verification Readiness](/resources/launch-readiness).

---

For the structural reasoning behind these trade-offs, read [Why Polaris?](/why-polaris). For the precise definition of any term above, see the [Glossary](/resources/glossary).

---

## 10-Minute Introduction

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/explainers/10-min

<div className="pl-rt-badge">10 min read · Full Introduction</div>

# 10-Minute Introduction

## Where stablecoins break down

DeFi has had two kinds of stablecoin: centralized-but-scalable and trustless-but-small.

The pattern is structural. A project starts with relatively clean collateral (crypto assets, onchain yield) and as it grows, it adds real-world assets, centralized stablecoins, or CEX-dependent yield to keep pace with demand. The economics push in that direction: T-bills and lending desks are large, reliable, and easy to integrate. Onchain alternatives are harder to scale.

The result: the largest stablecoins in DeFi are managed by entities that can freeze balances, blacklist addresses, and change collateral composition at will. They are useful, but they are not counterparty-free.

Meanwhile, the trustless designs — LUSD, BOLD — have proven that immutable CDPs work. But without a scalable onchain yield source, their growth is constrained, their rates are volatile, and they have remained in a trustless-focused niche.

The unresolved question is whether a stablecoin can be *both* trustless and scalable — one where the yield source itself grows with protocol adoption, not one borrowed from a centralized counterparty. Polaris is built to answer that question.

## The lineage

Polaris is not starting from scratch. It descends from a line of immutable, counterparty-free CDP stablecoins:

![Polaris counterparty-free CDP lineage: SAI (2017), LUSD (2021), BOLD / Liquity V2 (2024), pUSD (2026)](/infographics/polaris-lineage.svg)

- **SAI (2017):** The first single-collateral CDP stablecoin. Proved the model works. Later became multi-collateral DAI, then drifted toward centralized backing — Real World Assets, USDC in the PSM, governance-controlled rates.
- **LUSD (2021):** Immutable, counterparty-free, governance-free. Spent years with no admin breach. But capital-inefficient: it spent roughly 20% of its life more than 1% above peg, and its yield was constrained to redemption fee revenue.
- **BOLD (2024):** Liquity V2 — refined CDP mechanics, multi-collateral, still trustless. A genuine improvement, still yield-constrained.
- **pUSD (2026):** The next chapter. A bonding-curve collateral that solves the overpeg problem and produces yield that scales with DeFi volume, without adding a counterparty.

The lesson from the lineage is that immutability and counterparty-freedom are achievable and proven — SAI, LUSD, and BOLD demonstrated that. The missing piece has always been a scalable onchain yield source.

## The bonding curve: the engine of everything

The bonding curve is what makes Polaris different from every prior CDP stablecoin.

When you deposit ETH into Polaris, the curve issues you **pETH** at the current price. When you redeem pETH, you receive ETH back — at the floor price (at minimum), or the market price (if higher).

### The floor price

The floor price is the minimum exchange rate between pETH and ETH. It is mathematically guaranteed to only rise: each time swap fees or conversion fees accumulate, a portion adjusts the floor upward. There is no mechanism that lowers it.

This has a concrete implication: pETH is not a volatile asset. It is an ETH proxy whose downside is permanently bounded. The floor-to-market ratio — your maximum drawdown — shrinks monotonically over time as fees accumulate.

If pETH's market price is 0.95 ETH and its floor is 0.85 ETH, your worst-case redemption is 0.85 ETH. As fees accumulate, that floor may rise to 0.87, then 0.90. It never falls.

### Onchain-only yield

The curve earns fees on every ETH↔pETH swap. Those fees raise the floor. This means pETH's yield comes from trading volume — from real onchain activity — not from a T-bill desk or a CEX lending book.

This is the key insight: **the more DeFi activity flows through Polaris, the higher the floor rises, and the more valuable pETH becomes.** Yield scales with adoption instead of being capped by whatever a counterparty can deliver.

## pAssets: a factory of synthetic assets

With pETH as the collateral primitive, Polaris runs a factory of CDP (Collateralized Debt Position) instances. Each CDP issues a different synthetic asset against the same collateral:

- **pUSD** — pegged to the US dollar
- **pGOLD** — pegged to gold
- **pCHF** — pegged to the Swiss franc
- (additional pAssets are deployable as new CDP instances)

### How a CDP works

You lock pETH in a **trove** and mint a pAsset against it. Your collateral ratio must stay above the minimum threshold for your trove type. If it falls below, your trove is liquidated — the Stability Pool absorbs your debt and receives your collateral at a discount.

The peg is maintained by two opposing arbitrage forces:

**Below peg:** Anyone can bring pAssets to the protocol and redeem them for pETH at face value (minus a small fee). If the system is under-backed, redemptions are scaled down in proportion to the reserve backing. This burns pAssets, reduces supply, and drives the price back up.

**Above peg:** Anyone can open a trove, mint pAssets cheaply, and sell them above peg for a profit. This increases supply and drives the price back down.

### Autonomous interest rates

Polaris sets borrower interest rates without an external price oracle. Instead, it uses redemption and minting volume as proxy signals:

- High redemption volume → the pAsset is trading below peg → rates rise to deter new minting.
- Low redemption volume → the peg is healthy → rates fall to encourage minting.

This makes the rate-setting mechanism self-contained and tamper-proof.

### The Stability Pool

The Stability Pool is the safety net for every CDP. Holders deposit pAssets; when a trove is liquidated, the Stability Pool absorbs the debt and receives the collateral at a discount. Depositors earn:

1. A share of borrower interest from all CDPs.
2. Discounted pETH from liquidations.
3. POLAR emissions are planned for an incentive period (not yet enabled in the contracts).

It is the primary yield venue for pAsset holders and the mechanism that keeps the CDP system solvent.

## POLAR: stewardship through alignment

POLAR is the protocol's stewardship token. Apart from a one-time genesis allocation of 100,000,000 POLAR minted to the stewardship address at deployment, its issuance is deliberately constrained: **beyond that fixed initial supply, POLAR can only be minted by burning pETH in a conversion auction.** There is no other ongoing issuance path — no investor unlock and no inflationary mint function.

### What burning pETH in a conversion auction does

When pETH is burned:
1. The ETH backing the burned pETH is freed from the bonding curve's floor reserve — a configurable rebate goes back to the converter and the remainder is recycled into the system (market-buying pETH to refill the reward buffer).
2. The pETH floor price rises (supply contraction).
3. POLAR is minted to the burner.

All three effects are positive for the system. Burning pETH is the highest-alignment action a user can take.

### vePOLAR and stewardship

A designated stewardship role can steer quantitative parameters — such as fee and interest-rate settings and the conversion parameters — within the bounds the immutable contracts define. It cannot modify the bonding curve logic, CDP mechanics, liquidation rules, or the fixed collateralization thresholds.

This is a meaningful distinction: Polaris has stewardship, not governance. The core is fixed; only the dials within it are adjustable.

## The system end to end

![Polaris system: ETH enters the bonding curve as pETH, pETH collateralizes CDPs that mint pAssets, burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor and releases ETH](/polaris-system-v2.svg)

The four-step picture:
1. **Swap.** ETH enters the bonding curve and is issued as pETH at the current price.
2. **Collateralize.** pETH is the only collateral accepted across every CDP instance.
3. **Borrow.** A CDP mints a pAsset — pUSD, pGOLD, or any other — against that pETH.
4. **Stewardship.** Burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor, releases ETH, and aligns token holders with the protocol's long-term health.

## The flywheel

![Polaris flywheel: more adoption drives activity, activity generates fees, fees fund conversions, conversions raise the pETH floor, a higher floor strengthens collateral, stronger collateral enables more minting, which drives more adoption](/infographics/polaris-flywheel.svg)

> Adoption → activity → fees → conversions → higher pETH floor → stronger collateral → more minting → more adoption.

Each component reinforces the others:

- More pAsset CDPs create demand for pETH.
- Demand for pETH generates bonding curve swap fees.
- Fees raise the pETH floor.
- A higher floor makes every existing CDP position safer (the collateral is worth more in ETH terms).
- Safer positions lower the protocol's risk profile, attracting more users.
- More users generate more activity, and the loop continues.

This compounding is structural, not dependent on incentives or emissions.

## The three guarantees

| Guarantee | How it is enforced |
|---|---|
| **pETH floor only rises** | Bonding curve math — no mechanism in the contract can lower it |
| **No counterparty in the collateral stack** | pETH is backed by ETH; pAssets are backed by pETH; nothing else enters |
| **Immutable core** | Bonding curve, CDP logic, liquidations, and redemptions are fixed in bytecode; there is no admin key and no upgrade function for the core |

These are not design goals or promises. They are invariants enforced by the deployed contracts.

## Risk model

Understanding Polaris includes understanding what can and cannot happen:

**What can happen:**
- Your trove can be liquidated if your collateral ratio falls below the minimum. You retain any surplus above the liquidation threshold — liquidations are not a total loss.
- Recovery Mode is triggered when the protocol's reserve backing relative to total debt (its reserve-to-debt ratio) falls below a threshold. While active, borrowing tightens — actions that increase debt or reduce collateral must meet a higher minimum collateral ratio and may not worsen a position's ratio — but liquidation eligibility itself stays at the normal minimum collateral ratio, and pure collateral top-ups and repayments remain allowed.
- pETH's market price can fall (it tracks ETH), though the floor is guaranteed. If ETH falls sharply, the floor protects you from the worst of it.

**What cannot happen by design:**
- The pETH floor cannot be lowered.
- The CDP or bonding curve logic cannot be upgraded.
- Your pAsset balance cannot be frozen or confiscated.

---

Where to go next depends on what you want to do:

- Ready to use it? [Getting Started](/getting-started)
- Want the concept glossary? [Core Concepts](/core-concepts)
- Understand the bonding curve in depth: [The Bonding Curve](/peth/bonding-curve) and [The Floor Price](/peth/floor-price)
- Mint your first pUSD: [Open a Trove](/minting/open-a-trove)
- Earn yield: [The Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool)
- Build on Polaris: [Architecture](/developers/architecture)

    display: inline-block;
    font-size: 0.65rem;
    font-weight: 600;
    letter-spacing: 0.08em;
    text-transform: uppercase;
    padding: 3px 10px;
    border-radius: 20px;
    margin-bottom: 1rem;
    background: rgba(74,127,168,0.1);
    border: 1px solid rgba(74,127,168,0.3);
    color: #2c5f8a;
  }
  :is(.dark) .pl-rt-badge {
    background: rgba(123,165,201,0.12);
    border-color: rgba(123,165,201,0.35);
    color: #7ba5c9;
  }

---

## 2-Minute Overview

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/explainers/2-min

import { Callout } from 'nextra/components'

<div className="pl-rt-badge">2 min read · Overview</div>

# 2-Minute Overview

Polaris is a **self-scaling stablecoin operating system** — trustless, immutable infrastructure for minting synthetic assets backed entirely by onchain collateral and yield. No T-bills. No CEXs. No counterparties.

## One primitive, three products

Everything flows from a single primitive: the **bonding curve**.

**pETH** is ETH deposited into a two-way bonding curve. It is the only collateral Polaris accepts. Its floor price — the minimum price at which you can always redeem it back for ETH — can only rise. pETH is yield-bearing: every swap fee the curve earns raises that floor.

**pAssets** (pUSD, pGOLD, …) are synthetic assets minted against pETH via a CDP (Collateralized Debt Position). You lock pETH, the protocol mints a pAsset into your wallet. No counterparty holds your position. The logic is immutable.

**POLAR** is the stewardship token. There is only one way to create it: burn pETH in a conversion auction. vePOLAR (locked POLAR) steers protocol parameters within hardcoded bounds — it does not control the immutable core.

## The flywheel

The three products compound:

![Polaris flywheel: adoption drives activity, activity generates fees, fees enable conversions, conversions raise the pETH floor, a higher floor strengthens collateral, stronger collateral enables more minting](/infographics/polaris-flywheel.svg)

> More pAsset activity → fees → conversions → higher pETH floor → stronger collateral → more minting.

Growth reinforces the system rather than diluting it.

## Why it is different

- **Immutable core.** Bonding curve, CDP logic, liquidations, and redemptions are fixed in code. There is no admin key.
- **Onchain-only yield.** Every yield source (borrower interest, swap fees, conversion gains) is internal to the protocol and grows with DeFi activity.
- **Guaranteed floor.** pETH's floor can only rise. Your maximum downside on pETH is mathematically bounded and shrinks over time.

That is the whole system. Three products, one flywheel, one immutable core.

---

**Go deeper →** [5-Minute Presentation](/explainers/5-min) · [Core Concepts](/core-concepts) · [Getting Started](/getting-started)

<style>{`
  .pl-rt-badge {
    display: inline-block;
    font-size: 0.65rem;
    font-weight: 600;
    letter-spacing: 0.08em;
    text-transform: uppercase;
    padding: 3px 10px;
    border-radius: 20px;
    margin-bottom: 1rem;
    background: rgba(74,127,168,0.1);
    border: 1px solid rgba(74,127,168,0.3);
    color: #2c5f8a;
  }
  :is(.dark) .pl-rt-badge {
    background: rgba(123,165,201,0.12);
    border-color: rgba(123,165,201,0.35);
    color: #7ba5c9;
  }
`}</style>

---

## 5-Minute Presentation

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/explainers/5-min

<div className="pl-rt-badge">5 min read · Presentation</div>

# 5-Minute Presentation

## The problem

Stablecoins have always faced a structural trilemma: **scalable, yield-bearing, or trustless — pick two.**

The largest stablecoins are scalable and yield-bearing but rely on centralized issuers with freeze and blacklist powers. The trustless designs are counterparty-free but capital-inefficient and yield-constrained. The pattern is consistent: as a stablecoin scales, it drifts toward centralized yield because that is the path of least resistance.

Polaris is designed so that drift cannot happen. Its yield sources are *internal* to the protocol, growing with onchain activity. Its core logic is immutable, with no admin key able to add a counterparty under pressure.

## The counterparty-free lineage

Polaris descends from the line of immutable CDP stablecoins:

![Polaris lineage: SAI (2017) → LUSD (2021) → BOLD / Liquity V2 (2024) → pUSD (2026)](/infographics/polaris-lineage.svg)

Each generation fixed a flaw the previous hit. SAI proved CDPs could work; LUSD proved they could be immutable; BOLD refined the mechanism. Polaris extends the lineage by solving the *yield-scaling* constraint: the bonding curve converts trading volume directly into protocol revenue, so yield grows with adoption instead of being rented from a CEX.

## The three engines

### pETH — collateral with a rising floor

pETH is ETH deposited into a two-way bonding curve. When you deposit ETH, you receive pETH at the current price. When you redeem, you get back ETH at the floor price (at minimum).

Two things are guaranteed by the bonding curve's math:

1. **The floor price only rises.** As swap fees accumulate, the floor adjusts upward. There is no mechanism that can lower it.
2. **pETH is yield-bearing.** Every fee the curve earns narrows the gap between pETH's floor and its market price, real yield from real activity with no counterparty required.

pETH is the *only* collateral Polaris accepts. This is a feature: it means every pAsset is ultimately backed by ETH, with nothing else in the collateral stack.

### pAssets — synthetic assets from CDPs

pAssets (pUSD, pGOLD, pCHF, …) are synthetic assets minted against pETH collateral via CDPs. You lock pETH in a trove; the protocol mints a pAsset into your wallet.

The pAsset always trades near its peg because two forces bound it:

- **Below peg:** Anyone can redeem pAssets for pETH at face value, arbitraging the price back up.
- **Above peg:** Anyone can open a trove and mint more pAssets cheaply, arbitraging the price back down.

Interest rates adjust autonomously: redemption and minting volume are used as onchain proxy signals, with no external oracle needed. Polaris is a *factory* of CDPs: the same logic can issue pUSD, pGOLD, or any synthetic asset.

### POLAR — stewardship, not speculation

POLAR is the protocol's stewardship token. There is exactly one way to create it: burn pETH in a conversion auction. No team allocation, no investor unlock, no inflationary mint.

Burning pETH in a conversion auction does three things simultaneously:
1. Releases the pETH's backing ETH to the treasury.
2. Raises the pETH floor (supply reduction).
3. Mints POLAR to the converter.

Locking POLAR creates **vePOLAR**, which steers quantitative parameters (fee rates, thresholds) within ranges hardcoded into the immutable contracts. Stewardship, not governance.

## The system end to end

![Polaris system: ETH enters the bonding curve as pETH, pETH collateralizes CDPs that mint pAssets, burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor and releases ETH](/polaris-system-v2.svg)

Four steps:
1. **Swap.** ETH enters the bonding curve and is issued as pETH.
2. **Collateralize.** pETH is deposited as the sole collateral in a CDP.
3. **Borrow.** The CDP mints a pAsset (pUSD, pGOLD, or any other).
4. **Stewardship.** Burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor and releases ETH.

## The flywheel

![Polaris flywheel: adoption drives activity, fees enable conversions, conversions raise the floor, a higher floor strengthens collateral, stronger collateral enables more minting](/infographics/polaris-flywheel.svg)

> Adoption → activity → fees → conversions → higher pETH floor → stronger collateral → more minting.

Each component reinforces the others. More pAsset CDPs create demand for pETH; demand generates curve fees; fees raise the floor; a higher floor makes every existing position safer; safer positions attract more users.

## The three guarantees

| Guarantee | Enforcement |
|---|---|
| pETH floor only rises | Bonding curve math — no mechanism can lower it |
| No counterparty in the collateral stack | pETH is backed by ETH; nothing else enters |
| Immutable core | Bonding curve, CDPs, liquidations, and redemptions are fixed in code |

---
